- **Invented design intent** - the REST surface, consumer-claim, and signal vocabulary were stated as decided; they are now carried as flagged ASSUMPTIONS in the children (B1/B2, A1/A2, D1), overridable by the human.

### Epic-level scope (authoritative IN/OUT for the whole feature)

**IN (v1):** named work lists over REST (#273); GitHub issues as the item source; the terminal-signal contract (#272); the Gateway queue runner (#274); Cockpit and product-skill as clients (#275, #276); cross-machine parallelism (one list per machine).

**OUT (v1):** same-machine git-worktree parallelism (needs slot/build/port isolation that does not exist - Constraint 1); Azure DevOps work items as a second source; list persistence across a Gateway restart (in-memory is fine for v1); retry/auto-fix of a `failed`/`needs-human` item.

### Epic-level proof target

The epic is "done" when, on two machines, two named lists each drain unattended - each item gets one implementation session seeded with `/implementation-loop <N>`, advances only on the terminal signal, the Cockpit shows live per-item status from the `flow:*` labels, and the Director host carries no list/runner logic. That end-to-end demo is the sum of the five children's proofs; this epic is not independently `flow:ready-dev`.

### Assumptions (flagged, overridable by the human)

- Endpoints live on the existing Gateway API surface; item ref = bare GitHub issue number, single-repo v1 (#273 B1/B2).
- Terminal signal travels on the session transcript as a sentinel block (#272 A1/A2).
- Cockpit derives per-item badges from `flow:*` labels (#275 D1).

## Decision: the lists stay disconnected from trackers

After working through the cockpit UI, we locked a core architectural decision for this epic.

**The work lists never connect to GitHub, DevOps, or JIRA. The product agent is the only thing that touches a tracker.**

Rationale:

- **Runtime status needs no tracker connection.** During a run, an item's status (queued / running / done / needs-human / failed) comes from the loop's terminal signal, which the queue runner already observes. That is the runtime truth, and it comes from our own loop, not from a tracker.
- **Display fields come from the agent's stamped snapshot.** When the product agent appends an item it also writes a normalized shape: `{ source, id, url, title, area }`. So titles, areas, and links render with zero tracker calls.
- **Connecting would cost more than it gives.** A live connection only buys reflecting out-of-band tracker edits and browsing issues from inside the cockpit. Convenience, not necessity. The cost is GitHub + DevOps (+ later JIRA) SDKs and credentials living in the Gateway/Cockpit, three integrations to maintain, and a second status source that can drift from the trackers' own state. That fights the dumb-core, single-source-of-truth, PM-agnostic line of this epic.

Consequences for the design:

1. **The list is a tracker-agnostic priority spine.** `source` (github / devops / jira) is just a tag the agent stamps. Adding JIRA later is zero work in the list and the UI: one more `source` value the agent populates.
2. **Freshness, if ever needed, is an agent sync pass**, not a cockpit connector. The product agent already holds the tracker connection, so it re-stamps snapshots. Every tracker integration stays in exactly one place (the agent).
3. **No run-report page.** A per-list run report is redundant: the list view already shows each item's outcome. (A future cross-run "loop performance" view, fed only by the loop's own run records and still touching no tracker, is a possible later add. Out of scope for this epic.)

One line: the agents are the adapters, the list is the universal priority spine, the cockpit is the lens. No tracker logic crosses into the spine or the lens.

### UI mockup note
The Lists screen (cockpit nav, after Cockpit) shows freely-named priority lists on the left; each item row carries its own per-item `area`, `source`, `id`, `title`, and `status`. A single list mixes areas freely (it is not sliced by area).

EPIC COMPLETE - all 5 children verified and squash-merged to main by the Implementation loop (one DEV + one independent QA phase each, proof under docs/cencon/proof/issue-<n>/).

| Child | Title | Result | PR |
|-------|-------|--------|----|
| #273 | Named work lists REST resource (CRUD + ordering + claim) | flow:done | #278 |
| #272 | implementation-loop machine-readable terminal signal | flow:done | #281 |
| #274 | Gateway queue runner drains one named list | flow:done | #282 |
| #275 | Cockpit work-list client (create/reorder + watch status) | flow:done | #284 |
| #276 | product-agent skill appends ready-for-dev item over REST | flow:done | #285 |

The autonomous work-item queue runner is shipped end-to-end: named lists exist as a Gateway REST resource, the loop emits the IMPL-LOOP-TERMINAL sentinel, the queue runner drains a list one implementation session per item, the Cockpit is a pure client of the list object, and the product-agent can append items to a list. Closing this tracking epic.

title: [Gateway] Autonomous work-item queue runner: REST-API named work lists drained by the Gateway (EPIC)

body:
**Backbone of epic #279 (Remote-first Cockpit reliability).** Re-sharpened by the Product Agent to
the Definition of Ready on 2026-06-10. The product owner chose the **route-through-Gateway** approach
(this issue's original "Option B") over making each Director advertise a tailnet endpoint; this issue
is now scoped to that one fix. The original symptom write-up (loopback endpoint leaking to a remote
browser) is preserved under "Background / proof" below.

## Problem / Value

Opening the Cockpit from a machine that is **not** the Gateway host and using a per-session WebSocket
feature - **Speak/dictation** or the **live Terminal** - fails, because the browser is told to open a
WebSocket **directly to the owning Director's own advertised address**:

- dictation: `cockpit-dictate.js` connects to `DictateWsUrl(DirBase)`, `DirBase => Selected.TailnetEndpoint`
  (`src/CcDirector.Cockpit/Components/Pages/Cockpit.razor`, `DictateWsUrl` ~line 1434, `DirBase` ~1258);
- terminal: `TerminalPane.BuildWsUrl(DirectorBase, sid)` over the same base
  (`src/CcDirector.Cockpit/Components/TerminalPane.razor`, `BuildWsUrl` ~line 65).

When that advertised address is loopback (`http://127.0.0.1:7887`), the remote browser resolves it to
*its own* loopback and the upgrade never lands ("WebSocket connection failed."). Even when the
Director is tailnet-reachable, the browser must reach each Director directly, which is fragile and
the root reason voice + terminal do not work cross-machine.

The Gateway **already** reverse-proxies HTTP `/sessions/{sid}/*` calls to the owning Director and
passes the Blazor-circuit WebSocket through (`src/CcDirector.Gateway/Cockpit/CockpitProxy.cs`,
`src/CcDirector.Gateway/Api/GatewayEndpoints.cs`). The **two raw per-session WS legs are the only gap.**
Closing it means the browser only ever talks **same-origin to the Gateway**, and every per-session WS
feature works from any tailnet machine without depending on a Director advertising a correct address.

## Root cause (proven)

1. The browser builds the dictation/terminal WS URL from the Director's advertised `TailnetEndpoint`,
   so a loopback endpoint (verified in logs: `[GatewayEndpoints] verify 37cea6e2... endpoint=http://127.0.0.1:7887`)
   becomes `ws://127.0.0.1:7887/dictate` on a remote browser - its own loopback.
2. The Gateway forwards HTTP `/sessions/{sid}/*` to the owning Director (e.g. `MapDelete /sessions/{sid}`,
   `MapPost /sessions/{sid}/hold` in `GatewayEndpoints.cs`) and proxies the Cockpit + Blazor circuit
   (`CockpitProxy.cs`), but it does **not** proxy the WS legs `GET /dictate`
   (`src/CcDirector.ControlApi/DictationEndpoint.cs:103`) or `GET /sessions/{sid}/stream`
   (`src/CcDirector.ControlApi/TerminalStreamEndpoint.cs:53`). Confirmed: no `GET /dictate` ever
   reaches a Director from a remote client in the day's logs.

## Approach (decided: route through the Gateway)

The Gateway resolves the owning Director from the session registry and reverse-proxies the WS upgrade
to that Director, reusing the **same YARP `IHttpForwarder`** that already carries the Blazor-circuit
WS (so binary PCM audio frames and text frames pass through unchanged - YARP handles the upgrade).

1. **Gateway: new per-session WS proxy endpoints**
   - `GET /sessions/{sid}/stream` - resolve the owning Director by `sid` from `DirectorRegistry`,
     forward to `{director.TailnetEndpoint}/sessions/{sid}/stream` via `IHttpForwarder.SendAsync`
     (the `CockpitProxy.CockpitForwarder.ForwardAsync` pattern, but with a per-request destination
     computed from the registry instead of the fixed loopback Cockpit).
   - `GET /sessions/{sid}/dictate` - **new sid-scoped path at the Gateway** that resolves the owning
     Director by `sid` and forwards to that Director's existing `{director.TailnetEndpoint}/dictate`
     (the Director endpoint is unchanged; the Gateway introduces the sid in the path so it can pick
     the right Director). See Assumption A1.
   - Both must sit ahead of the `MapFallback` Cockpit proxy and the browser-page routes so they win.
   - Resolve "owning Director for sid" with the existing registry helper used by the other
     `/sessions/{sid}/*` forwards (`DirectorRegistry` + `DirectorEndpointClient`); if the session is
     unknown, return `404`; if the owning Director is offline, return `503` (do not hang).
2. **Cockpit: build both WS URLs same-origin against the Gateway, not the Director**
   - `DictateWsUrl`: target the Gateway origin the Cockpit page was served from
     (`NavigationManager.BaseUri` / `window.location`), path `/sessions/{sid}/dictate`
     (`http(s)` -> `ws(s)`). Stop using `Selected.TailnetEndpoint` for this URL.
   - `TerminalPane.BuildWsUrl`: same - target the Gateway origin, path `/sessions/{sid}/stream`.
     `DirectorBase` is no longer the WS host (it may remain for non-WS calls, but the WS URL must be
     same-origin). See Assumption A2.
3. **Loud failure when a WS cannot be established** (carries forward this issue's original ask #2):
   the Cockpit must distinguish "could not reach the Gateway proxy" from "the Gateway could not reach
   the owning Director" using the proxy's HTTP status (404/503) or close code, and show that instead
   of a bare "WebSocket connection failed." (The deep dictation error-UX - typed Director error,
   retry-with-audio - is the separate child #226, gated on this issue.)

## Scope

**IN**
- Two new Gateway per-session WS proxy endpoints (`/sessions/{sid}/stream`, `/sessions/{sid}/dictate`)
  that resolve the owning Director and reverse-proxy the upgrade via the existing forwarder.
- Cockpit change: dictation and terminal WS URLs become **same-origin to the Gateway**.
- A clear remote-failure message distinguishing Gateway-unreachable vs Director-unreachable.
- Unit test(s) for the URL builders (Gateway-origin + sid path + http->ws / https->wss + trailing slash).
- Gateway-side log lines for each proxied WS leg (open, resolved Director, close) for diagnosability.

**OUT**
- The dictation error semantics + retry-with-preserved-audio (child **#226**, depends on this).
- Cockpit persisted logging (child **#199**, independent, already ready-dev).
- Any visual redesign; any change to the Director's `/dictate` or `/sessions/{sid}/stream` handlers
  themselves (they are unchanged - only the Gateway and the Cockpit URL building change).
- Making Directors advertise a tailnet endpoint (the rejected alternative - this fix removes the need).

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] **Terminal cross-machine:** from a non-Gateway machine, opening the Terminal tab for a session
      owned by a *remote* Director shows live output. The browser's WS connects to
      `wss://<gateway-host>/sessions/{sid}/stream` (Gateway origin), **never** to a Director address.
      (Proof: browser devtools/network screenshot showing the WS URL is the Gateway host + a Director
      log line showing the forwarded `GET /sessions/{sid}/stream` arriving from the Gateway, not the browser.)
- [ ] **Dictation cross-machine:** from a non-Gateway machine, clicking Speak on a remote-Director
      session records and transcribes; the WS is `wss://<gateway-host>/sessions/{sid}/dictate`.
      (Proof: successful transcription screenshot + the WS URL in devtools is the Gateway host + the
      Director's `[DictationEndpoint] GET /dictate` log line shows the request arriving from the Gateway.)
- [ ] **No loopback ever:** with a Director that advertises `http://127.0.0.1:7887`, neither WS URL
      built by the Cockpit contains `127.0.0.1`/`localhost`; both target the Gateway origin.
      (Proof: unit test asserting the builder output + a devtools screenshot against such a Director.)
- [ ] **Unknown session -> 404, offline Director -> 503:** requesting `/sessions/<bad-sid>/stream`
      returns 404; requesting a stream for a session whose Director is offline returns 503 within a
      few seconds (no hang). (Proof: two `curl`/REST responses.)
- [ ] **Loud failure:** when the Gateway cannot reach the owning Director, the Cockpit shows a message
      that names that cause (Director unreachable), not a bare "WebSocket connection failed."
      (Proof: screenshot with the owning Director stopped.)
- [ ] **Same-machine unchanged:** on the Gateway host itself, dictation and terminal still work
      (the same-origin URL resolves to the Gateway, which proxies to the local Director). (Proof:
      screenshot on the Gateway machine.)
- [ ] **Unit tests green:** WS-URL builder tests (Gateway origin, sid path, http->ws/https->wss,
      trailing slash) pass.

## Affected Containers

- `CcDirector.Gateway` - new per-session WS proxy endpoints + registry resolution + forwarder reuse
  (`Api/GatewayEndpoints.cs` or a new `Api/SessionWsProxyEndpoints.cs`; `Cockpit/CockpitProxy.cs`
  forwarder pattern).
- `CcDirector.Cockpit` - `Components/Pages/Cockpit.razor` (`DictateWsUrl`), `Components/TerminalPane.razor`
  (`BuildWsUrl`), and the JS error handling in `wwwroot/js/cockpit-dictate.js` for the loud-failure case.
- `CcDirector.ControlApi` - **read-only**: the Director's `/dictate` and `/sessions/{sid}/stream`
  handlers are unchanged (verify they accept a forwarded upgrade from the Gateway; they already
  accept any WS upgrade).

Must comply with `docs/CodingStyle.md` and `docs/VisualStyle.md` (the loud-failure message is a UI change).

## Proof Target

One HTML report committed under `docs/cencon/proof/issue-268/` containing, all captured **from a
non-Gateway machine over the tailnet**:
1. Devtools/network screenshots proving both WS URLs are the **Gateway** origin (no Director address).
2. A successful remote **dictation** transcription and a live remote **Terminal** tab.
3. Director-side log lines showing `/dictate` and `/sessions/{sid}/stream` arriving **from the Gateway**.
4. The 404 (bad sid) and 503 (offline Director) REST responses.
5. The loud-failure dialog screenshot (owning Director stopped).
6. Green unit-test output for the WS-URL builders.

## Assumptions (flagged - overridable by the human)

- **A1:** The dictation WS gets a new **sid-scoped path at the Gateway** (`/sessions/{sid}/dictate`)
  so the Gateway can pick the owning Director; the Director's own endpoint stays `/dictate`. If the
  developer finds a cleaner scheme (e.g. `/directors/{id}/dictate`), that is acceptable provided the
  browser still targets the Gateway origin and never a Director address.
- **A2:** `DirectorBase`/`TailnetEndpoint` may remain for non-WS HTTP calls; only the **WS URL** must
  become same-origin. The developer may leave a `// TODO #267-followup`-style note where a Director
  address is still used for non-WS calls, but must not change that here.
- **A3:** YARP's `IHttpForwarder` proxies a WebSocket upgrade (including binary frames) transparently,
  as it already does for the Blazor circuit. If a forwarder limitation surfaces for raw WS, a manual
  bidirectional pump (`AcceptWebSocketAsync` on the Gateway + `ClientWebSocket` to the Director,
  copying both directions preserving frame type) is the fallback - developer's call, same acceptance
  criteria either way.

---

## Background / proof (original symptom write-up, retained)

Opening the Cockpit from a different machine than the Gateway host and clicking Speak on a session
owned by the co-located Director failed almost immediately with "WebSocket connection failed."
(`cockpit-dictate.js` `ws.onerror`). The owning Director `37cea6e2` advertised a loopback-only
endpoint:

```
05:54 [GatewayEndpoints] verify 37cea6e2... callbackOk=True, endpoint=http://127.0.0.1:7887   <- loopback only
05:54 [GatewayEndpoints] verify a0d9f4a3... callbackOk=True, endpoint=https://sorenlaptop.taildb08ed.ts.net:7880  <- proper tailnet
```

Cockpit assets loaded fine over the tailnet, but **no `GET /dictate` request exists anywhere in the
day's Director or Gateway logs** - the upgrade never reached a server. This is the mechanism behind
#198 (blank Terminal) and is distinct from #226 (a *reachable* remote Director whose WS connects then
drops ~1.4s in - that issue's error-UX + retry work is gated on this backbone).

### Related
- #279 - parent epic (Remote-first Cockpit reliability).
- #226 - dictation error UX + retry-with-audio (depends on this).
- #198 - blank Terminal / stream WS (same leg; verify resolved by this).
- #199 - Cockpit logging discarded (why this was hard to diagnose; independent, already ready-dev).
- #267 - Open-in-Cockpit button uses configured gateway URL (sibling, already ready-dev).

comments.body: Re-sharpened to the Definition of Ready as the **backbone of epic #279**. Product owner chose the route-through-Gateway approach (this issue's original Option B) over per-Director tailnet-endpoint advertisement. DoR 7/7; labeled `flow:ready-dev`. Title/body rewritten to the Gateway-proxy spec; original symptom write-up retained under Background.

## Developer Agent - Ready for QA

Proof: docs/cencon/proof/issue-268/report.html  (PR #283)

### Release Notes
A remote Cockpit can now use dictation + the live Terminal for sessions owned by any tailnet Director. The two raw per-session WS legs are now reverse-proxied through the Gateway; the Cockpit builds both WS URLs same-origin to the Gateway, never to a Director address.

### Changes
- Gateway `Api/SessionWsProxyEndpoints.cs` (new): `GET /sessions/{sid}/stream` + `/dictate`, resolve owning Director, reverse-proxy the upgrade via the shared YARP `IHttpForwarder`; 404 unknown session, 503 unreachable Director; mapped ahead of the fallback proxy.
- Gateway.Contracts `CockpitWsUrls.cs` (new): pure, unit-tested same-origin WS-URL builder (http->ws/https->wss, sid path). Used by `TerminalPane`, `Cockpit.razor`, `BriefPane`.
- `cockpit-dictate.js`: loud failure naming "Director unreachable through the Gateway".
- CenCon `architecture_manifest.yaml`: both new components registered.

### How to Test
1. From a non-Gateway machine, open the Cockpit over the tailnet, pick a session owned by a remote Director, open Terminal + click Speak.
2. Devtools: the WS URL must be `wss://<gateway-host>/sessions/{sid}/{stream|dictate}` (no Director address).
3. `GET /sessions/<bad>/stream` -> 404; stop the owning Director -> 503 + loud Cockpit message.

### Expected Result
Live remote Terminal output and a successful remote transcription, WS always on the Gateway origin; Director logs show the forwarded `/dictate` and `/sessions/{sid}/stream` arriving from the Gateway.

### Before / After
- Before: `ws(s)://<director-endpoint>/...` (loopback breaks a remote browser).
- After: `ws(s)://<gateway-origin>/sessions/{sid}/...` (Gateway proxies to the owner).

### Proof captured (single machine)
- Full solution build 0 warnings / 0 errors (SDK 10.0.301 - see build note).
- 18 new unit tests green (URL builders + wire 404/precedence); full Gateway suite 362/362.
- Live `GatewayHost` on loopback: both legs return `404 {"error":"session not found across any director"}` (never the Cockpit interstitial), with `[SessionWsProxy]` open/resolve/404 log lines.

### Not proven here (flagged for QA)
The two cross-machine criteria (remote devtools WS-origin screenshot, remote transcription, Director receiving the forwarded upgrade) need a second tailnet box + a live mic, unavailable in this session. Every mechanism they depend on is proven above. Please run the two-box capture to close them.

### Build note
Local SDK 10.0.103 has a Razor regression mis-parsing a relational-pattern switch arm in the pre-existing `Cockpit.razor` (fails identically on clean `main`; CI's 10.0.x compiles it 0/0). Proof produced with SDK 10.0.301.

## QA Agent — PARKED for human (flow:needs-human)

Independent QA on a single machine. I never reused the Developer's binary or screenshots; I checked out `issue-268-gateway-ws-proxy`, built and ran the tests myself, and read the code.

### What I verified independently (PASS)
- **AC#7 Unit tests green** — `dotnet test CcDirector.Gateway.Tests` → **366/366 pass**, including the **18 new** tests (`CockpitWsUrlsTests` + `SessionWsProxyEndpointsTests`). No regression.
- **AC#3 (unit-test half) No loopback ever** — `CockpitWsUrls` builder proven (own run) to emit `ws(s)://<gateway-origin>/sessions/{sid}/{stream|dictate}` and **never** `127.0.0.1`/`localhost`; http→ws / https→wss; trailing-slash + deep-link path dropped; non-default port preserved.
- **AC#4 (404 + route precedence)** — `SessionWsProxyEndpointsTests` drives a **real `GatewayHost`** over loopback: unknown sid on both `/stream` and `/dictate` returns **404** (`{"error":"session not found…"}`), and the explicit routes **win over the fallback Cockpit proxy** (no "Cockpit starting" interstitial). Verified live, not from the dev report.
- **Code review** — wiring is correct: `IHttpForwarder` is registered (`GatewayHost.cs:221 AddHttpForwarder()`), `SessionWsProxyEndpoints.Map` is mapped ahead of the fallback proxy, all three Cockpit call sites (`TerminalPane`, `Cockpit.razor`, `BriefPane`) now build the WS URL via `CockpitWsUrls` from `Nav.BaseUri` (never a Director address), `_Imports.razor` imports the namespace, and `cockpit-dictate.js` shows the loud "Could not reach the owning Director through the Gateway" message with a correct `wasReady` open/mid-stream split. No DT-rule / CenCon violation; manifest updated.

### Why I cannot close it myself — two independent blockers
1. **Cross-machine criteria are unprovable on one machine.** AC#1 (remote Terminal), AC#2 (remote dictation + live mic), the devtools-screenshot halves of AC#3/#4, AC#5 (loud-failure dialog), and AC#6 (same-machine-on-Gateway) all require the issue's mandated proof — *"captured from a non-Gateway machine over the tailnet"* — i.e. a **second tailnet box and a live mic**, which this session does not have. The Developer flagged exactly this and asked QA to run the two-box capture. I have the same single-machine constraint.
2. **Local SDK cannot build the Cockpit to even launch it.** SDK **10.0.103** (mine) fails the Cockpit Razor build with `RZ10007`/`RZ9980`/`RZ1024` parser errors in the pre-existing `Cockpit.razor`. **I independently confirmed clean `origin/main` fails identically** (worktree build, same errors, same region) — so this is a pre-existing SDK 10.0.103 Razor regression, **not** introduced by PR #283 (CI on 10.0.x compiles it). But it means I cannot produce a clean full-solution build, which the PASS-path merge gate requires, nor run the Cockpit locally to drive the UI criteria.

### Decision
No defect found — the code, tests, and wiring are sound. I will not PASS criteria I cannot prove, and there is nothing to FAIL. This is the textbook **needs-human** case: a human (or a two-box QA run on a CI-grade SDK) must capture the cross-machine Terminal/dictation/loud-failure proof and do the merge on a clean full-solution build.

**PARKED:** PR #283 left open with all work committed. Working tree clean, no stash. The repo is otherwise as clean as `main`.

**PR #283 rebased onto current `main`** (was 7 commits behind, cut before the Cockpit relational-pattern compile fix #286).

State now:
- Builds clean: `dotnet build cc-director.sln` → **0 errors, 0 warnings** (the Cockpit project compiles again).
- Tests green: Gateway **404 passed**, Cockpit **12 passed**, 0 failures.
- PR is **MERGEABLE** onto `main`.

The remaining blocker is unchanged and **requires a second tailnet machine** (cannot be done by the single-machine autonomous loop): acceptance criteria AC1 (cross-machine Terminal) and AC2 (cross-machine Dictation) require proof captured *"from a non-Gateway machine over the tailnet"* per the Proof Target. The branch is now current and buildable so that two-box QA can proceed.

## ✅ Two-box QA — VERIFIED live, cross-machine

The fix (PR #283, merged to `main` as `e388446`) was deployed to the **Gateway host SOREN_NORTH** (`soren-north.taildb08ed.ts.net`) and verified from a **second physical machine, SORENLAPTOP** (`100.89.217.71`) — the proof angle the single-machine loop couldn't reach.

| AC | Result |
|----|--------|
| AC1 Terminal cross-machine | ✅ `wss://<gateway>/sessions/<sid>/stream` → 101 + live frames |
| AC2 Dictation routing cross-machine | ✅ `wss://<gateway>/sessions/<sid>/dictate` → 101 + `{"type":"ready"}` |
| AC3 No loopback | ✅ both URLs are the Gateway origin, never a Director address |
| AC4 Unknown session → 404 | ✅ live 404 from the new proxy endpoint |
| Director-side forwarding | ✅ Director logs both legs `from 127.0.0.1` (the Gateway), **not** the browser `100.89.217.71` |
| AC7 Unit tests | ✅ Gateway 404 + Cockpit 12 green |
| AC4 503 / AC5 loud-failure / AC6 same-machine | unit-tested + same proven proxy path; live captures deferred (need stopping a live Director) |

**Director log (SOREN_NORTH):**
```
16:52:00.385 [TerminalStreamEndpoint] GET /sessions/<sid>/stream from 127.0.0.1
16:52:28.063 [DictationEndpoint] GET /dictate from 127.0.0.1
```

Full report: `docs/cencon/proof/issue-268/two-box-qa-report.html` (commit `9653f8f`).

The four criteria that genuinely required a second machine pass live. Marking `flow:done`.

**AC5 (loud-failure) now also captured live, cross-machine.** Drove SOREN_NORTH's Cockpit from a browser on SORENLAPTOP; the real Speak flow with the dictate WS forced to a non-existent session renders the named cause — *"Could not reach the owning Director through the Gateway (it may be offline or unreachable)."* — not a bare "WebSocket connection failed." Screenshot + updated report: `docs/cencon/proof/issue-268/ac5-loud-failure.png` (commit `8ca5f3a`). Only AC4-503 remains unit-only (its live capture needs briefly stopping a live Director).

**Correction on AC4-503 (and my earlier unit-only claim).** Trying to capture the 503 live surfaced a real finding: an **offline owning Director returns 404, not the spec'd 503**.

The proxy resolves ownership via `GetSessionAsync` on the Director *before* forwarding, so resolution requires the Director to be reachable. Every Director in the fleet has resolve endpoint == forward endpoint (`37cea6e2` loopback-only; `a0d9f4a3` ControlEndpoint == TailnetEndpoint), so an offline Director fails at resolution → 404. The 503 branch (resolve-OK + distinct dead ForwardDestination) is unreachable in normal operation, and `SessionWsProxyEndpointsTests` does not actually cover it (its comment defers 503 to the proof run).

I won't fake a 503 screenshot. Filed **#288** to either align the spec (offline → 404) or resolve-from-registry-then-503. Report corrected (commit `4b9bf05`). This doesn't affect the AC5 loud-failure UX, which fires correctly regardless (the browser can't read 404 vs 503 off a failed upgrade).

Net for #268: **6/7 ACs verified live cross-machine; AC4-503 is a spec/impl discrepancy tracked in #288**, not a pass.

title: [Cockpit] Route per-session WebSocket legs (dictate + terminal stream) through the Gateway so a remote Cockpit never needs a Director's address

body:
## Problem / Value

A Director configured to use a **remote** gateway cannot open the Cockpit from the toolbar. Clicking the **Cockpit** button (top-right of the main Avalonia window) always probes `http://127.0.0.1:7878` (localhost), ignoring the gateway URL the user already configured in Settings. When no gateway runs on this machine, the click fails with a modal:

> **Cannot Open Cockpit** - Could not reach the local gateway at http://127.0.0.1:7878: No connection could be made because the target machine actively refused it. Is the Gateway tray app (cc-director-gateway) running on this machine?

The Director is registered with its remote gateway and knows exactly where it is - but the button asks localhost anyway. This breaks the Cockpit front door for every cross-machine / tailnet setup (seen live on SORENLAPTOP pointed at `http://soren-north.taildb08ed.ts.net:7878`). The fix makes the button honor the one configured source of truth.

## Root cause

`BtnCockpit_Click` hardcodes the gateway base URL to `DirectorView.DefaultGatewayUrl` (`http://127.0.0.1:7878`) and never consults the configured gateway URL from `GatewayConfig`.

- `src/CcDirector.Avalonia/MainWindow.axaml.cs` - `BtnCockpit_Click` builds the request URL as `Controls.DirectorView.DirectorView.DefaultGatewayUrl + "/cockpit"` and interpolates the same constant into both error dialogs.
- `src/CcDirector.Avalonia/Controls/DirectorView/DirectorView.axaml.cs:16` - `public const string DefaultGatewayUrl = "http://127.0.0.1:7878";`.
- The configured/registered URL exists and is loaded elsewhere but is never used here: `src/CcDirector.Core/Configuration/GatewayConfig.cs` exposes `GatewayConfig.Load()` -> `.Url` / `.IsEnabled`, read from the `gateway` block of `config.json`; the Settings dialog writes it (`src/CcDirector.Avalonia/SettingsDialog.axaml.cs`).
- An established resolution pattern already exists: `src/CcDirector.ControlApi/ControlApiHost.cs` does `gatewayConfig.IsEnabled ? gatewayConfig.Url : null`.

The `/cockpit` endpoint returns the correct front-door URL for whichever gateway answers - it computes the Tailscale front door from that gateway machine's own identity (`src/CcDirector.Gateway/Api/GatewayEndpoints.cs`). Querying the remote registered gateway returns that gateway's Cockpit URL, which is exactly what we want. `/cockpit` is on the public no-auth allowlist (`src/CcDirector.Gateway/Util/AuthMiddleware.cs`), so no bearer token is required. No gateway-side or auth change is needed.

This is selection by configuration, not a fallback chain: one configured source of truth (`gateway.url`), used everywhere; loopback is used only when no gateway URL is configured at all (correct for same-machine setups).

## Scope

**IN**
- `BtnCockpit_Click` in `src/CcDirector.Avalonia/MainWindow.axaml.cs`: resolve the gateway base URL from `GatewayConfig.Load()` (configured -> use `.Url`; not configured -> loopback default) before building the `GET {base}/cockpit` request.
- Trailing-slash safety so a configured `http://host:7878/` yields `http://host:7878/cockpit` (not `//cockpit`).
- Both failure dialogs report the URL **actually** probed; the "Is the Gateway tray app running on this machine?" hint appears **only** when the probed URL was the loopback default. For a remote gateway the hint is about reachability (gateway down / tailnet).
- Update the handler's leading comment block to say it asks the **configured** gateway (falling back to local `127.0.0.1:7878` only when no gateway URL is configured).
- Keep existing log lines but include the resolved `baseUrl` for traceability.
- Unit tests for the URL-resolution logic (extract a small testable `static string ResolveCockpitBase(GatewayConfig cfg)` helper if needed).

**OUT**
- `DirectorView._gatewayUrl` (`DirectorView.axaml.cs:21`) also defaults to `DefaultGatewayUrl` and is never reassigned from `GatewayConfig`, so the sessions list likewise talks only to localhost. That is a separate, broader centralization issue and is **not** fixed here. (Developer Agent may leave a `// TODO #267-followup` note but must not change that behavior in this issue.)
- No changes to the gateway side, `/cockpit` endpoint, or auth middleware.
- The remote-Director WS loopback-advertisement bug (#268) and Speak-dies-cross-machine (#226) are unrelated code paths and out of scope.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] **Remote gateway configured:** with `gateway.url = http://<remote>:7878` in `config.json` and no local gateway listening on `127.0.0.1:7878`, clicking the Cockpit button issues `GET http://<remote>:7878/cockpit` and opens the front-door URL that endpoint returns. (Proof: Director log line showing `baseUrl=http://<remote>:7878` + browser opens the remote Cockpit URL.)
- [ ] **No gateway configured:** with no `gateway.url` set, behavior is unchanged - the button probes the loopback default `http://127.0.0.1:7878/cockpit`. (Proof: log line showing `baseUrl=http://127.0.0.1:7878`.)
- [ ] **Trailing slash:** a configured `http://host:7878/` produces the request URL `http://host:7878/cockpit` (verified by unit test `ResolveCockpitBase` trailing-slash case).
- [ ] **Error dialog accuracy:** when the probe fails, the dialog text names the exact URL that was probed (not a hardcoded `127.0.0.1:7878`), and the "Is the Gateway tray app running on this machine?" sentence appears only when the probed URL was the loopback default.
- [ ] **No localhost ever opened in the browser:** the browser is never navigated to a `127.0.0.1`/`localhost` Cockpit URL when a remote gateway is configured (existing invariant, kept).
- [ ] **Unit tests pass:** `ResolveCockpitBase_UrlConfigured_ReturnsConfiguredUrl`, `ResolveCockpitBase_NoUrl_ReturnsLocalhostDefault`, and the trailing-slash case are present and green.

## Affected Containers

- `CcDirector.Avalonia` - `MainWindow.axaml.cs` (`BtnCockpit_Click`), unit tests for the resolution helper.
- `CcDirector.Core` - **read-only**: reuses existing `Configuration/GatewayConfig.Load()`; no change expected.

## Proof Target

A single HTML report (committed to the PR branch under `docs/cencon/proof/issue-267/`) containing:
1. A screenshot or captured Director log showing the Cockpit button, with a **remote** `gateway.url` configured, issuing `GET http://<remote>:7878/cockpit` (the `baseUrl=` log line visible) and the browser opening the remote front-door URL.
2. The error-dialog screenshot for a remote gateway that is down, showing the **remote** URL named in the message and the local-only hint absent.
3. The green unit-test run output for the three `ResolveCockpitBase` tests.

## Assumptions

- The `/cockpit` endpoint returns the answering gateway's own front-door URL and is anonymous-allowlisted - confirmed from current source (`GatewayEndpoints.cs`, `AuthMiddleware.cs`), not invented.
- `GatewayConfig.IsEnabled` is true exactly when a non-empty `gateway.url` is configured - matches the existing `ControlApiHost` usage pattern.
- No design decision is left open; if the Developer Agent finds the `ResolveCockpitBase` helper cannot be unit-tested without touching UI code, extracting it into `CcDirector.Core` or a small static in the Avalonia project is acceptable (developer's call), provided the three tests exist.

---

_Sharpened by the Product Agent to the Definition of Ready. Supersedes #238 (closed as duplicate); #238's acceptance points (error-dialog hint local-vs-remote, never open a localhost URL) are folded into the Acceptance Criteria above._

comments.body: Part of epic #279 (Remote-first Cockpit reliability). Already `flow:ready-dev` - sibling of the backbone #268; independent of it (this is the Avalonia toolbar button, not the WS legs).

## Developer Agent - Ready for QA

Proof: docs/cencon/proof/issue-267/report.html  (PR #292)

### Release Notes
The Cockpit toolbar button now honors the configured gateway URL instead of always probing `127.0.0.1:7878`. Cross-machine / tailnet Directors can open the Cockpit from the toolbar.

### Changes
- New `CockpitUrlResolver.ResolveCockpitBase(GatewayConfig)` (UI-free, unit-tested): configured `gateway.url` (trailing slash stripped) or the loopback default.
- `MainWindow.BtnCockpit_Click`: resolves base from `GatewayConfig.Load()`, builds `{base}/cockpit`, logs `baseUrl=` everywhere, dialogs name the probed URL; local-machine hint only for loopback, tailnet hint for remote.
- `DirectorView._gatewayUrl` left unchanged with a `// TODO #267-followup` (OUT of scope).
- Three `ResolveCockpitBase` unit tests.

### How to Test
1. `gateway.url` = remote, no local gateway -> click Cockpit -> log `baseUrl=http://<remote>:7878`, browser opens the remote front door.
2. No gateway block -> click -> log `baseUrl=http://127.0.0.1:7878` + loopback error dialog (local-machine hint).
3. Unreachable remote gateway -> click -> dialog names the remote URL with the tailnet hint (local hint absent).

### Expected Result
Configured gateway probed; loopback only when none configured; accurate error dialogs; no localhost URL ever opened in the browser for a remote setup.

### Before / After
Before: always `http://127.0.0.1:7878/cockpit` -> "Cannot Open Cockpit" on every cross-machine setup.
After: probes the configured `gateway.url` (verified live -> `baseUrl=http://soren-north.taildb08ed.ts.net:7878`, opened `https://soren-north.taildb08ed.ts.net/`).

Build: `Build succeeded. 0 Warning(s) 0 Error(s)`. Tests: 3/3 ResolveCockpitBase green (38/38 in the project). Verified live on a slot-5 test Director driven through the real toolbar button via UI Automation.

## QA Agent - VERIFIED (flow:done)

Independent verification on a freshly built **slot-5** test Director (`Build succeeded. 0 Error(s)`), launched via the `cc-director-launch` scheduled task, driving the **real Cockpit toolbar button** through Windows UI Automation (`BtnCockpit`). I edited the Director's `config.json` gateway block per scenario and restored it byte-for-byte afterward.

**All 6 acceptance criteria PASS:**

| AC | Result | Proof |
|----|--------|-------|
| AC1 remote configured | PASS | Log: `baseUrl=http://soren-north.taildb08ed.ts.net:7878` then `opening https://soren-north.taildb08ed.ts.net/ (up=True)` - probed the configured remote gateway and opened its front door |
| AC2 no gateway | PASS | Log: `baseUrl=http://127.0.0.1:7878` (loopback default, unchanged) |
| AC3 trailing slash | PASS | `ResolveCockpitBase_TrailingSlash...` green: `http://host:7878/` -> `http://host:7878/cockpit` |
| AC4 dialog accuracy | PASS | Loopback dialog: names `http://127.0.0.1:7878` + "...running on this machine?". Remote-down dialog: names `http://unreachable-remote...:7878` + tailnet hint, local hint absent |
| AC5 no localhost opened | PASS | Remote case opened only the remote front door; loopback case opened nothing |
| AC6 unit tests | PASS | 3/3 `ResolveCockpitBase` tests green |

**Regression/method sweep:** scope honored (`DirectorView._gatewayUrl` left with the `// TODO #267-followup` note, OUT of scope); no gateway/`/cockpit`/auth change; selection-by-configuration not a fallback chain; solution builds clean.

Proof report: `docs/cencon/proof/issue-267/qa-report.html` (+ `qa/qa-ac4-remote-down-dialog.png`).

VERIFIED - all acceptance criteria met. Squash-merging to main.

title: [Cockpit] Open-in-Cockpit toolbar button must use the configured gateway URL, not hardcoded 127.0.0.1:7878

body:
## Problem / Value

`SessionStatusWingmanTests.PromptInjectionWatcher_pushes_extracted_text_via_wingman_source` is timing-flaky and currently `[Fact(Skip)]`, so the prompt-injection -> wingman-source push path has **no regression coverage**. The watcher does one debounced scan ~500 ms after bytes arrive (`PromptInjectionWatcher.OnTimerTick` reading `SnapshotScreenRowsWithCursor`); if that single scan reads a grid/cursor state that yields no push, it never re-scans (no further bytes arrive in the test), so the push never lands - intermittently, worse under CI load. Documented dead ends: longer polling (single scan still misses), re-emitting the frame (changes the resolved grid -> different value), NUL-byte nudge (fires the push but the session's own input sync sets `PendingPromptText` with source `"user"`, racing the `"wingman"` push and breaking the `PendingPromptText` assert).

## Scope

**IN**
- Rewrite the test to deterministically drive exactly one scan against a known-resolved grid, using the seam that **already exists**: `PromptInjectionWatcher.RequestImmediateScan()` (`src/CcDirector.Core/Wingman/SessionStatusWingman.cs:340`).
- Confirm the grid is resolved, call `RequestImmediateScan()`, and assert on the captured **"wingman"-source** event (the `captured` value) rather than `PendingPromptText` (which the session also sets with source `"user"`, causing the race).
- Remove the `[Fact(Skip)]`. Reach the internal `PromptInjectionWatcher` via the existing `InternalsVisibleTo`.

**OUT**
- Changing production watcher behavior or timing.
- The broader parallelization work (already done in #263 / closed #233).

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] The test is no longer skipped and asserts on the `"wingman"`-source captured event, not `PendingPromptText`. (Proof: the diff.)
- [ ] It passes deterministically - green across **50 consecutive runs** locally (looped `dotnet test --filter ...` or an xUnit repeat). (Proof: the 50/50 green run output.)
- [ ] It contains no fixed `Task.Delay`-then-assert; the single scan is driven via `RequestImmediateScan()` after the grid is confirmed resolved. (Proof: the diff.)

## Affected Containers

- `CcDirector.Core.Tests` - `Wingman/SessionStatusWingmanTests.cs`.
- `CcDirector.Core` - `Wingman/SessionStatusWingman.cs` (test seam only; **no production behavior change** - `RequestImmediateScan()` already exists).

## Proof Target

`docs/cencon/proof/issue-264/`: the un-skipped test green across 50 consecutive runs.

## Assumptions

- `RequestImmediateScan()` provides the deterministic single-scan trigger the rewrite needs (confirmed present at `SessionStatusWingman.cs:340`). None invented.

comments.body: Claimed by an implementation-loop run. Manual duplicate-prevention (#298 not built yet): removing flow:ready-dev so no concurrent loop selects this issue while implemented. Transitions to flow:ready-qa then flow:done per normal flow; flow:ready-dev restored if the run aborts.

## Developer Agent - ready for QA

Proof: docs/cencon/proof/issue-264/report.html  (PR #308)
50-run log: docs/cencon/proof/issue-264/50x-green.txt

### Release Notes
Re-enables regression coverage of the prompt-injection -> wingman-source push path; the flaky skipped test is now deterministic. No production behavior change.

### Changes
- Rewrote `SessionStatusWingmanTests.PromptInjectionWatcher_pushes_extracted_text_via_wingman_source`: un-skipped, drives the internal `PromptInjectionWatcher` directly, confirms the grid resolves before triggering, fires one scan via the existing `RequestImmediateScan()` seam, awaits the `"wingman"`-source push via a `TaskCompletionSource`, and asserts on the captured wingman value (not `PendingPromptText`).

### How to Test
```
dotnet build cc-director.sln
dotnet test --no-build --filter FullyQualifiedName~PromptInjectionWatcher_pushes_extracted_text_via_wingman_source
```

### Expected Result
Build clean. Test green deterministically: 50/50 consecutive PASS, 0 skipped (verified locally - see 50x-green.txt). Full class: 37 passed, 0 skipped.

### Acceptance criteria
- [x] Un-skipped; asserts on the `"wingman"`-source captured event, not `PendingPromptText`.
- [x] Green across 50 consecutive runs (50/50).
- [x] No fixed `Task.Delay`-then-assert; single scan driven via `RequestImmediateScan()` after the grid is confirmed resolved.

## QA Agent - PASS (independent verification)

Independently verified on a fresh checkout of PR #308 (did not reuse the Developer Agent's binary or 50x log).

### Acceptance criteria - 3/3 PASS
- [x] Un-skipped; asserts on the `"wingman"`-source captured event, not `PendingPromptText`. (Diff: `[Fact(Skip)]` -> `[Fact]`; final assert is on the TCS result set only when `source == "wingman"`; live run reports `Skipped: 0`.)
- [x] Deterministic across 50 consecutive runs - my own loop: PASS=50, FAIL=0 of 50.
- [x] No fixed `Task.Delay`-then-assert; grid confirmed resolved first, single scan via `RequestImmediateScan()`, awaited through a `TaskCompletionSource` (the 10s `Task.WhenAny` is a hang guard only).

### Regression / method
- Production `SessionStatusWingman.cs` UNTOUCHED (empty diff); `RequestImmediateScan()` already existed at line 340. No production behavior change.
- Full `SessionStatusWingmanTests` class: 37 passed, 0 skipped, 0 failed.
- Merge gate: `dotnet build cc-director.sln` -> Build succeeded, 0 Warning(s), 0 Error(s).
- CenCon: no drift (test-only, existing internal seam).

Proof: `docs/cencon/proof/issue-264/qa-report.html`

VERIFIED - all acceptance criteria met. Merging.

title: [Tests] Deterministic rewrite of the flaky PromptInjectionWatcher wingman-source test

body:
## Summary

Revise the New Session dialog and session-type model so that **Developer and QA are no longer two separate sessions**. They become roles inside **one Implementation session** that loops build<->verify until the task is done. The Product group shrinks from four tied sessions to two: **Product + Implementation**. Support leaves the group but stays selectable as a solo type.

This revises the design shipped in #254 (which made the Product group four members: Product / Developer / QA / Support).

## Why

- **Same-repo parallelism doesn't work.** Two sessions on one repo fight over the working tree, build output, the running app, ports, and slots. You can't reliably test a build while another agent is mid-edit on the same checkout.
- **Dev<->QA is a tight loop, not a one-way handoff.** QA finds a defect -> dev fixes -> QA re-verifies -> repeat. Across two sessions every turn costs a label handoff, a context reload, and a re-orientation. In one session the loop keeps full context and runs fast.
- **Single owner of the working tree.** The Implementation session is the only thing that edits, builds, and runs the app - no coordination protocol between two agents over one filesystem.
- **Simpler human model.** Product (decide + specify) -> Implementation (build + verify, looping) -> ship.

## Design / mockup

Before/after mockup (before = #254 design, after = this change), with the full type legend and rationale:

[docs/plans/new-session-dialog-implementation-merge.html](docs/plans/new-session-dialog-implementation-merge.html)

## Scope - what changes

1. **`src/CcDirector.Core/Sessions/SessionType.cs`**
   - Add a new `Implementation` member. Give it a playbook that drives the internal developer<->QA loop: write the code, then put on the QA hat and verify against what was asked, fix what fails, re-verify, loop until done; this session owns the working tree, builds, and runs the app.
   - Keep `Developer`, `QA`, and `Support` defined and selectable (solo use, and so persisted sessions still load). Keep integer values stable; add a legacy-name alias if the chosen integer collides, following the existing `SessionTypeNames.TryParse` / `SessionTypeJsonConverter` pattern.
   - `Implementation` becomes the default type offered in the picker.

2. **`src/CcDirector.Core/Sessions/SessionGroupDefinition.cs`**
   - Product group `BuiltIn` shrinks from 4 members to 2: `Product` (" - product") then `Implementation` (" - implementation"). Update the group `Description`.

3. **New Session dialog (`src/CcDirector.Avalonia/NewSessionDialog.axaml` + `.axaml.cs`)**
   - Type picker lists the types per the mockup: primary = Implementation (default), Product, Discuss; advanced/below-divider = Developer, QA, Support.
   - The Product group preview shows the 2 sessions that will be created.
   - "Start N Sessions" button reflects the new count (2).

4. **Badge + color for Implementation** (`SessionViewModel.cs` and wherever type badges/colors are mapped)
   - Add the `[I]` Implementation badge in the teal shown in the mockup (`#14b8a6`). Developer keeps its existing default/gray treatment.

5. **CenCon doc (`docs/cencon/DEVELOPMENT_METHOD.md`)** - light edit
   - The four agent roles and the flow:* labels are unchanged.
   - Rewrite the session-topology lines (around lines 25, 52, 83-84): Developer and QA run as roles inside one Implementation session, looping over flow:ready-dev / flow:ready-qa internally.
   - Soften the SOC 2 "the QA session is a different identity from the developer session" line to role separation within one session.

## Out of scope

- Building the actual in-session dev<->QA loop automation (the Implementation playbook describes the loop; wiring any auto-orchestration of flow:* within the session is a separate issue).
- Changing the Gateway REST contract beyond carrying the new enum value (the new type must round-trip through the Gateway's `NewSessionRequest` deserialization - confirm it does).
- Touching the archived WPF dialog under `archived/`.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] New Session dialog Type picker shows Implementation as the default, plus Product and Discuss as primary, with Developer/QA/Support available below an "advanced/solo" divider, matching the mockup.
- [ ] Selecting "Group: Product" previews exactly two sessions (Product, Implementation) and the start button reads "Start 2 Sessions".
- [ ] Creating the Product group produces two sessions named `<repo> - product` and `<repo> - implementation` with the correct types and seeded playbooks.
- [ ] An Implementation session starts with a playbook that instructs the build-then-verify loop and single-tree ownership.
- [ ] A session persisted under an old type name (e.g. `Developer`, `QA`, `Support`, and the #254 legacy `Implement`/`BugReport`) still loads without error.
- [ ] The Implementation type shows the `[I]` teal badge on the rail; Developer keeps its prior default treatment.
- [ ] `DEVELOPMENT_METHOD.md` describes Developer+QA as roles inside one Implementation session and no longer claims a separate QA session identity.
- [ ] Unit tests: `SessionGroupDefinition` Product group = 2 members in order; `SessionTypePlaybooks.ComposeSeed` for Implementation; serialization round-trip + legacy-alias load.

## Test plan

- Unit tests as above (green).
- Build `cc-director.sln`, launch a slot-5 test Director via the `cc-director-launch` scheduled task, open New Session, verify the Type dropdown and the 2-session Product group preview, create the group, and confirm both sessions appear with correct names/types/badges. Capture a screenshot + HTML proof report under `docs/cencon/proof/issue-<n>/` on the PR branch (CenCon method).

## Notes

- Back-compat: stable integer enum values + legacy-name aliases, exactly as #254 established.
- This is the design of record going forward; the #254 four-member group is superseded.

comments.body:
## Developer Agent - Implemented, ready for QA

Branch `feat/issue-259-implementation-merge` (PR #262).

**Proof:** `docs/cencon/proof/issue-259/report.html` (PR #262) - live slot-5 screenshots.

### Acceptance criteria
| Criterion | Result |
|-----------|--------|
| Type picker: Implementation default; Product/Discuss primary; Developer/QA/Support under Advanced divider | PASS (Fig 1) |
| Group "Product" previews 2 (Product, Implementation); button "Start 2 Sessions" | PASS (Fig 2) |
| Group creates `<repo> - product` + `<repo> - implementation`, correct types, one groupId | PASS (Fig 3 + API) |
| Implementation playbook = build-then-verify loop + single-tree ownership | PASS (unit test + live session) |
| Old type names still load (Developer/QA/Support + #254 Implement/BugReport) | PASS (theory tests) |
| `[I]` teal rail badge; Developer keeps prior treatment | PASS (Fig 3) |
| `DEVELOPMENT_METHOD.md` reflects Developer+QA as roles in one Implementation session | PASS |
| Unit tests (group=2 order; ComposeSeed; serialization + legacy alias) | PASS (50/50 in area) |

### Notes for QA
- Edge case covered: `"Implement"` (legacy alias -> Developer) is deliberately NOT confused with the new `"Implementation"` type - both have explicit tests.
- The four CenCon roles and `flow:*` labels are unchanged; only the session topology moved (Developer+QA now loop inside Implementation).
- Full `cc-director.sln` build is blocked only by the running cockpit's file lock (rule 0); all changed projects compile and tests pass.

Branch commits authorized; **not merged to main** (human merge per the method).

Merged to main via PR #262 (commit `2a9d5fd`), on the strength of the code review + slot-5 proof (same bar as #256). Leaving the label at flow:ready-qa - close it / mark flow:done if you consider the proof sufficient, or have QA re-verify the dialog live.
Merged to main (PR #262, commit 2a9d5fd) and verified via the code review + slot-5 proof. Marking flow:done and closing.

title: New Session dialog: merge Developer + QA into one Implementation session (revises #254)

body:
## Problem / Value

CC Director has a full installer (`tools/cc-director-setup`) but **no uninstall**. To remove it today you would hand-delete folders and still strand system-level artifacts the installer created outside the app tree - a user PATH entry, a Gateway autostart Run key, scheduled tasks, and Tailscale Serve mappings - plus you would have no safe way to stop the running Directors/Gateway first. There is no single place to say "remove everything CC Director put on my machine." This issue adds a first-class, UI-initiated uninstall that removes the Director, Gateway, Cockpit, the cc-* tools, the skills, and all integration points, while keeping the user's personal data by default.

## The install footprint (what must be removable)

| # | Artifact | Where | Placed by |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Director app + Gateway tray + Cockpit assets | `%LOCALAPPDATA%\cc-director\app\` (and retired `bin\`) | EngineInstallRunner |
| 2 | cc-* tools (one shared Python venv) | under `%LOCALAPPDATA%\cc-director\` | PythonToolsInstaller |
| 3 | User PATH entry -> venv shims | `HKCU\Environment\Path` | PathManager |
| 4 | Skills | `%USERPROFILE%\.claude\skills\<name>\` | SkillInstaller |
| 5 | Start Menu shortcut | `...\Programs\CC Director.lnk` | ShortcutCreator |
| 6 | Gateway autostart | `HKCU\...\CurrentVersion\Run` | GatewayApp Autostart |
| 7 | Scheduled tasks | `cc-director-launch`, `cc-director-gateway-launch` | setup/agent tooling |
| 8 | Tailscale Serve mappings | tailscaled (443 -> loopback) | Gateway (TailscaleServeProvisioner) |
| 9 | Personal data | `%LOCALAPPDATA%\cc-director\` -> config, logs, vault/keyvault.json (secrets), connections/ (signed-in browser sessions), snapshots, comm-queue DB, recordings | runtime |
| 10 | Running processes | the user's multiple Directors + Gateway tray | runtime |

## Design (one source of footprint truth, two front-ends)

- **Removal runs in the existing `cc-director-setup` wizard in a new `--uninstall` mode**, so uninstall is the *inverse* of install and reuses the engine's footprint knowledge (no separately-maintained delete list that drifts).
- **Each installer service gains an `Uninstall()` inverse:** PythonToolsInstaller, SkillInstaller, ShortcutCreator, PathManager, GatewayApp Autostart, a scheduled-task remover, a Tailscale-serve teardown, and the app-tree remover.
- **Self-deletion handoff:** the in-app UI launches the uninstaller **detached**, the app + Gateway exit, and the uninstaller removes the app tree and itself last (same pattern as the auto-update stage-then-apply). A running app cannot delete its own exe.
- **InstallDetector** drives the inventory so only artifacts that are actually present are acted on.

## Scope

**IN:**
- New `--uninstall` mode in `cc-director-setup` + the inverse `Uninstall()` ops listed above.
- In-app UI entry: **Tools -> Uninstall CC Director** -> inventory/confirm screen -> launches the detached uninstaller and exits.
- Discoverability: a Windows **Add/Remove Programs** entry (`HKCU\...\Uninstall\CCDirector`) and a Start Menu **Uninstall CC Director** shortcut, so it works even if the app will not launch.
- Safe shutdown of running Directors/Gateway with a roster snapshot first (ties into #246).
- "Also delete my data" opt-in (default OFF) and a removal report.

**OUT:**
- Uninstalling shared prerequisites we did not exclusively install (Tailscale itself, the Python runtime, winget-installed deps).
- Cross-machine / fleet uninstall (this machine only).
- Removing Cockpit as a separately-uninstallable unit (it is part of the app/Gateway tree).

## Affected Containers (per docs/cencon/architecture_manifest.yaml)

- **`tools/cc-director-setup` + `tools/cc-director-setup-engine`** - the `--uninstall` mode and the inverse `Uninstall()` operations. NOTE: the setup tool/engine is **not currently a container in architecture_manifest.yaml**; if this ships, the Support Agent should add it (CenCon doc gap, see Assumption 7).
- **`avalonia_ui`** (CcDirector.Avalonia) - the Tools-menu entry, the inventory/confirm dialog, the detached-launch + app-exit handoff.
- **`core_services`** (CcDirector.Core) - reuse of the session-roster snapshot (#246) and the Control-API graceful-stop path before removal.

No CenCon architecture/security drift beyond the new container note. The uninstall **reduces** the security surface (removes secrets only on explicit opt-in).

## Acceptance Criteria (each QA-verifiable by screenshot/inspection)

1. **Discoverable in three places.** An in-app **Tools -> Uninstall CC Director** menu item exists; a Windows **Add/Remove Programs** entry "CC Director" exists; a Start Menu **Uninstall CC Director** shortcut exists. Verify: screenshots of all three.
2. **Inventory screen lists only what is present.** The confirm screen itemizes the footprint actually found on the machine (the table categories) with real paths; categories not present are not listed. Verify: screenshot of the inventory screen.
3. **Data kept by default.** "Also delete my data (vault secrets, signed-in browser sessions, recordings, snapshots)" is shown, **unticked by default**, with a clear warning. Verify: screenshot of the confirm screen showing the default-off state.
4. **Never force-kills (Rule 0).** With Directors/Gateway running, uninstall detects them, writes a session-roster snapshot, and prompts the user to close them or offers a graceful Control-API stop - it never silently `taskkill`s. Verify: screenshot of the running-process prompt + the written snapshot file.
5. **Program + integration fully removed (data-kept run).** After a default uninstall, ALL of these are gone: the app tree, the shared venv + cc-* tools, the PATH entry, the cc-director-owned skills, the Start Menu shortcut, the autostart Run key, the scheduled tasks, the Tailscale Serve mappings, and the Add/Remove Programs entry. `%LOCALAPPDATA%\cc-director` data (config, vault, connections, recordings, snapshots) REMAINS. Verify: before/after of PATH, the Run key, `schtasks`, `tailscale serve status`, the app dir, and the data dir.
6. **Full wipe on opt-in.** With "also delete my data" ticked, `%LOCALAPPDATA%\cc-director` is removed entirely in addition to everything in AC5. Verify: after-state showing the directory gone.
7. **Self-delete completes.** The running app and Gateway exit and the program tree (including the app exe and the uninstaller itself) is fully removed - nothing of the program remains. Verify: after-state listing of `%LOCALAPPDATA%\cc-director\app`.
8. **Only cc-director skills removed.** Uninstall removes ONLY the skills CC Director installed, never the user's own/global skills in `%USERPROFILE%\.claude\skills`. Verify: a `.claude/skills` listing before/after showing user-authored skills untouched. (See Assumption 3 - this depends on an install manifest of owned skills.)
9. **Idempotent + report.** Missing artifacts are reported as skipped (not errored), and a removal report lists each item removed / kept / failed-with-reason (e.g., a file locked by a Director the user chose not to close). Verify: screenshot/text of the report.
10. **Builds clean.** `dotnet build cc-director.sln` and the setup tool build succeed with 0 errors; any new engine tests pass. Verify: build/test output.

## Proof Target

The Developer Agent's proof report (committed under `docs/cencon/proof/issue-<n>/`) must contain screenshots demonstrating, at minimum:
1. The three discovery entry points (AC1).
2. The inventory/confirm screen with data-keep default OFF (AC2, AC3).
3. The running-process safe-shutdown prompt + the roster snapshot file (AC4).
4. Before/after evidence for PATH, the autostart Run key, scheduled tasks, and `tailscale serve status` (AC5).
5. After-state proving the program tree is gone but `%LOCALAPPDATA%\cc-director` data remains (AC5), plus a separate full-wipe run showing the data dir gone (AC6).
6. A `.claude/skills` before/after proving user-authored skills are untouched (AC8).
7. The removal report (AC9) and the clean build output (AC10).

Plus an explicit "I believe this is finished" statement.

## Assumptions (flagged, not confirmed - resolve in implementation)

1. The existing `cc-director-setup` wizard is the right host for `--uninstall` (vs a new standalone tool). It already owns the engine and footprint knowledge.
2. The installer should ALSO begin writing an **Add/Remove Programs** registration on install going forward; for machines already installed without it, the uninstaller self-registers (or the Start-Menu/in-app entry covers them). Confirm we can write `HKCU\...\Uninstall\CCDirector` at install time.
3. **RISK - verify FIRST: skill ownership is tracked.** AC8 (do not delete the user's own skills) is only safe if SkillInstaller recorded which skill names it installed (an install manifest). If no such record exists, the Developer must add one - blindly deleting by a hardcoded name list is fragile and could remove a user skill that shares a name. If ownership cannot be determined safely, bounce scope back here rather than risk the user's `.claude/skills`.
4. Scheduled tasks `cc-director-launch` / `cc-director-gateway-launch` may have been created by agent tooling rather than the installer; uninstall removes them if present, regardless of creator.
5. The Tailscale-serve teardown removes only CC Director's mappings (the Gateway's 443 -> loopback), never other `tailscale serve` config on the machine.
6. Keep-data default means a later reinstall finds the vault keys and signed-in browser sessions intact.
7. The setup tool/engine is not yet a CenCon container; the Support Agent adds it to `architecture_manifest.yaml` if this ships (does not block the Developer).

Related: #242 (no-silent-startup / update health-check + rollback - same detached-handoff pattern), #243 (cc-launcher), #246 (proactive session snapshot - reused for the safe-shutdown step).

comments.body:
## Developer Agent - Phase 1 implemented (NOT ready-qa)

This issue is large and its ACs 4-7 require a **real, destructive** uninstall (wipes PATH, scheduled tasks, Tailscale Serve, app tree, and on full-wipe vault secrets + signed-in browser sessions). On the maintainer's production machine with no isolated VM, that proof is not safely runnable. Per maintainer direction, I implemented **Phase 1 = engine + CLI inverse operations, proven by unit tests + a sandbox**, with the skill-ownership question resolved by **adding an ownership manifest**.

**Proof:** `docs/cencon/proof/issue-257/report.html` (PR #258)

### What Phase 1 delivers (engine `tools/cc-director-setup-engine`, all tested)
- `SkillManifest` - records owned skills; uninstall removes ONLY those, never the user's own (AC8).
- `ScheduledTaskRemover` - `cc-director-launch` / `cc-director-gateway-launch` (Assumption 4).
- `TailscaleServeTeardown` - the 443 front-door mapping only (Assumption 5).
- `AddRemovePrograms` - register/unregister the HKCU Uninstall entry.
- `Uninstaller` wiring + `Plan()` inventory; CLI `uninstall` / `--dry-run` surface it.

138/138 engine tests pass, incl. a **real** schtasks round-trip on a throwaway task, a **real** HKCU registry round-trip on a throwaway key, and the AC8 only-our-skills guard. The app tree / venv / PATH / shortcut / autostart removal already existed in the engine.

### Why I am NOT moving this to flow:ready-qa
ACs 1-4, 6, 7 (in-app Tools->Uninstall UI, Start Menu uninstall shortcut, install-side ARP-register + skill-manifest-write call-sites, safe-shutdown roster snapshot, detached self-delete, and the real before/after destructive run) are **Phase 2** and not implemented. Marking the whole issue ready-qa would misrepresent it.

### Recommended next step (Product Agent)
Split #257 into:
1. A **Phase 1** item (this PR) covering the engine/CLI inverse ops - ready to verify against the Phase 1 ACs (AC5 mechanics, AC8, AC9, AC10).
2. A **Phase 2** item for the in-app UI + discoverability call-sites + safe-shutdown + self-delete, with its destructive end-to-end proof to run on a throwaway VM.

Leaving the label as `flow:ready-dev` for the Product Agent to re-scope.

## QA route: human, cross-machine (NOT the automated QA agent)

Per maintainer decision: #257's uninstall is destructive and machine-global, so the automated QA agent on the dev machine cannot verify it. The agreed plan instead:

1. **Thorough code review** of PR #258 (in progress - see finding below), plus optionally a cloud `/code-review ultra` pass.
2. **Push a full release** (accepting that this bypasses the automated QA gate).
3. **Test the uninstall on a separate, disposable machine** where wiping the install is fine - the real before/after destructive proof the sandbox couldn't give (PATH, scheduled tasks, Tailscale Serve, skills, ARP, app tree).
4. **Then test on this machine** specifically that the **Gateway** uninstall works (autostart Run key + Tailscale 443 teardown + gateway/cockpit dirs).

Labeling `flow:needs-human` (out of the dev/qa agent queues) so a human drives this; it is a deliberate cross-machine-QA route, not a 3-strike escalation.

### Code-review finding already fixed (pushed to PR #258, commit c977997)
`Uninstaller.RemoveSkills` deleted `Path.Combine(skillsDir, <manifest entry>)` recursively with no validation. A blank manifest entry resolves to the skills dir itself - it would have recursively deleted the user's **entire `~/.claude/skills` tree**; a `..\x` entry would escape it. The install path only writes `cc-director` today, but a destructive uninstaller must not trust a persisted manifest. Now guarded at the point of deletion (refuse any entry whose resolved parent is not exactly the skills dir) with a hostile-manifest regression test. Engine tests: 139/139.

When the release is built, the cross-machine test command is `cc-director-setup-cli uninstall --role workstation` (then `--role gateway` for the Gateway box / this machine), with `uninstall --dry-run` first to preview.

## Added: setup-wizard Uninstall button (PR #258)

Per maintainer follow-up, the practical uninstall entry point is the existing `cc-director-setup` wizard (no in-app Director menu or Start Menu shortcut needed yet). When the wizard runs and `InstallDetector.IsInstalled()` is true (update mode), the Welcome step now shows an **"Uninstall CC Director"** button -> confirm -> `Uninstaller.Apply(role)` -> result. Role auto-detected (Gateway only when a Gateway install is present). Data under the per-user root is preserved.

Also wired the one install-side hook needed for it to be useful: `SkillInstaller` now records installed skill names via `SkillManifest.RecordInstalled`, so the button actually removes the `cc-director` skill on a machine installed from this release.

Proof: `docs/cencon/proof/issue-257/report.html` (Fig 2 is a live capture of the button in update mode; the destructive path was not triggered on the dev machine). Cross-machine test path unchanged: run the wizard's Uninstall button (or `cc-director-setup-cli uninstall`) on a disposable machine, then the Gateway uninstall here.

Still Phase 2 / not done: in-app Director Tools->Uninstall, Start Menu shortcut, Add/Remove Programs registration, safe-shutdown roster snapshot, detached self-delete, and the real destructive proof.

## Merged to main

Per maintainer decision (code review serves as the gate; real destructive testing happens post-release on a disposable machine):

- **PR #258 merged** -> `main` (commit `90b976b`): Phase 1 engine/CLI inverse ops + the RemoveSkills safety hardening + the setup-wizard **Uninstall CC Director** button.
- The accidental repo-cleanup bundling was split out and merged separately (**PR #260**, commit `e17a1c5`).

**What is on main now:** the wizard shows an Uninstall button when it detects an install; it runs the engine `Uninstaller.Apply(role)` (skills via ownership manifest, scheduled tasks, Tailscale 443 teardown for Gateway, app/tools/PATH/shortcut/autostart), preserving user data. 139/139 engine tests green.

**Remaining (not done):**
- Real before/after **destructive validation on a disposable machine** (the wizard Uninstall button, then the Gateway uninstall here) - this was always the cross-machine test.
- **Phase 2**: in-app Director Tools->Uninstall menu, Start Menu uninstall shortcut, Add/Remove Programs registration, safe-shutdown roster snapshot, detached self-delete.

Leaving this issue open for the cross-machine validation + Phase 2. Close it if you consider Phase 1 sufficient, or I can open a dedicated Phase 2 issue.

Phase 2 tracked in #261 (in-app Tools->Uninstall menu, Add/Remove Programs registration, Start Menu shortcut, safe-shutdown + roster snapshot, detached self-delete, and the data-wipe opt-in). Phase 1 (this issue / PR #258) remains open only for the real cross-machine destructive validation.

Set to flow:ready-qa, but this is **human cross-machine QA** - the automated QA Agent should NOT attempt to verify it (a real uninstall is destructive and machine-global; it would wipe the dev box). Verification = run the wizard Uninstall button / CLI on a disposable machine, then the Gateway uninstall here.

Marking complete. The uninstall shipped in v0.6.21 (engine + setup-wizard button, PR #258) and was polished in v0.6.22 (in-wizard confirm -> live progress -> completion, PR #265). Maintainer will do the final destructive cross-machine verification on a disposable box later. Phase 2 (in-app menu, Add/Remove Programs registration, self-delete, data-wipe opt-in) is tracked separately in #261.
