**Setup (no disruption to the running fleet):** an isolated Gateway on :8978 built from this branch (version-stamped `0.6.22+4a791a0`) plus this branch's Cockpit on :7471. The isolated Gateway discovered the already-running Director (:7887) via the shared FSW instances dir, so the test ran against real sessions and a real terminal stream. Only :8978 was bounced; the live fleet (7878/7470/7887) was never touched.

| # | Test | Result |
|---|------|--------|
| 1 | Terminal connects + WS proxy through the gateway | Live content rendered (not blank) |
| 2 | **Real gateway restart -> recovery (headline)** | Kill :8978 -> Blazor "Rejoining" overlay -> relaunch -> terminal repopulated live, **no blank-forever** |
| 3 | **Fix A** - fresh page reload re-mounts + connects | Pane connected on mount and streamed |
| 4 | **Fix B** asset is the one served | served `cockpit-terminal.js` (via gateway origin) contains `MAX_RECONNECT_ATTEMPTS`, `statusLine`, reconnect/gave-up text |
| 5 | **Fix B** - visible status, circuit alive | Forced a stream-only failure -> terminal showed `[stream lost, reconnecting to 127.0.0.1:8978 (attempt 3)...]` while Blazor stayed up; restored -> recovered, status cleared |

**Method notes (honest):**
- A full gateway kill also drops the Blazor circuit (both legs proxy through the gateway), so during the outage Blazor's own "Rejoining the server..." overlay shows. That is the faithful restart condition; the meaningful assertion - the terminal recovers afterward rather than staying blank - passed.
- On localhost the stream replay returns in <40ms, so under a healthy reconnect the status line flashes too fast to capture. #198's real condition (a stream that will not reconnect) was reproduced by pointing reconnects at a dead port - the status line then persisted and was captured verbatim. On a genuine slow/hung remote link it persists naturally.

Net: no blank-forever after a gateway restart (Fix A), and a hung stream now shows visible, bounded reconnect feedback instead of a silent blank pane (Fix B). Screenshots captured locally during the run.

Fixed in PR #287 (squash-merged to main as 2c9427f). Both client-resilience defects addressed: the one-shot/DirectorBase-gated connect (blank-forever) and the silent unbounded reconnect (no on-screen state). Live-verified on a real Director stream across a real Gateway restart - terminal recovers (no blank-forever) and a hung stream now shows visible, bounded reconnect feedback. Verification screenshots attached to the PR. Disk-side WS logging remains with companion #199.

**Reproduce-check pending** (gate satisfied: backbone #268 is now `flow:done`). Half 1 of this issue ('stream WS never reaches the Director' cross-machine) is fixed by #268's Gateway-routed terminal stream (`/sessions/{sid}/stream` now proxied). Before re-sharpening the residual half 2 ('TerminalPane is one-shot + silent reconnect loop, no connecting state') to `flow:ready-dev`, someone must confirm on the post-#268 build whether the Terminal tab still goes blank / fails to recover from a non-Gateway machine. If it still reproduces, the Product Agent re-sharpens the client-resilience half (connecting/reconnecting visual state + bounded auto-reconnect on the pane). If it no longer reproduces after #268, this issue can be closed with that note. No `flow:*` label until the reproduce-check resolves.

title: Cockpit: Terminal tab can stay blank for minutes; stream WS never reaches the Director (one-shot TerminalPane mount + silent reconnect loop, no connecting state)

body:
## Symptom

The Cockpit triage rail shows the laptop permanently parked in UNREACHABLE:

> SORENLAPTOP / director a0d9f4a3 -- unreachable (timeout; cooling down)

The laptop is up, on the tailnet (direct connection, 5 ms tailscale ping from soren-north), and its Director process is alive and heartbeating. It never becomes reachable.

## Evidence (production Gateway log, 2026-06-06, PID 93492)

Heartbeats laptop -> gateway succeed every 15 s; every gateway -> laptop probe times out:

```
10:13:30 POST /directors/a0d9f4a3.../heartbeat -> 200 (0ms) client=100.89.217.71
10:13:33 [DirectorEndpointClient] ListSessionsAsync FAILED: endpoint=https://sorenlaptop.taildb08ed.ts.net:7879, error=HttpClient.Timeout of 2 seconds elapsing
10:14:03 [DirectorRegistry] a0d9f4a3... circuit OPEN after 10 failures; skipping for 30s (timeout)
10:15:39 [DirectorRegistry] Sweeper evicted unreachable http entry: a0d9f4a3... (control endpoint dead 200s, still heartbeating)
10:15:45 POST /directors/a0d9f4a3.../heartbeat -> 410
10:15:45 POST /directors/register: id=a0d9f4a3..., endpoint=https://sorenlaptop.taildb08ed.ts.net:7879, machine=SORENLAPTOP
10:15:45 [TailscaleServeProvisioner] HandleAdded: not mapping id=a0d9f4a3... (machine=SORENLAPTOP, control=https://sorenlaptop.taildb08ed.ts.net:7879) - only local Directors on ports 7879-7898 get a serve mapping
```

Network check from soren-north confirms the laptop is fine but nothing listens on the advertised port:

```
tailscale status:  100.89.217.71 sorenlaptop ... active; direct 192.168.1.3:41641
tailscale ping:    pong from sorenlaptop in 5ms
Test-NetConnection sorenlaptop.taildb08ed.ts.net -Port 7879 -> False (no listener)
```

## Root cause: nothing provisions tailscale serve on a workstation-only machine

The advertised endpoint depends on a serve mapping that no component ever creates on the laptop:

1. The Director binds Kestrel to **loopback only** (`ControlApiHost.cs` ~line 118-122). By design the raw port is never on the LAN or the Tailscale interface; the SOLE remote path is Tailscale Serve (HTTPS).
2. The Director advertises `https://<magicdns>:<port>` to the Gateway (`GatewayClient.ResolveTailnetEndpoint`, `src/CcDirector.ControlApi/GatewayClient.cs` ~line 280). The comment there says: "Remote reachability is provided by the Gateway's TailscaleServeProvisioner, which runs `tailscale serve --https=<port>` ... per Director."
3. But since issue #179, `TailscaleServeProvisioner.ShouldMap` (`src/CcDirector.Gateway/Tailscale/TailscaleServeProvisioner.cs` ~line 271) only maps Directors **on the Gateway's own machine** -- correctly, because a serve mapping proxies to `http://localhost:<port>` on the machine that runs the CLI; mapping a remote Director would point at a dead local port.
4. SORENLAPTOP is a Workstation-role install: no Gateway runs there, so **nobody ever runs** `tailscale serve --bg --https=7879 http://localhost:7879` **on the laptop**. The Director advertises an endpoint that cannot work by construction.

So the laptop Director registers a structurally-dead endpoint, the Gateway's 2 s probes time out (Tailscale delivers the SYN to the laptop, where nothing listens on 7879 and the firewall drops it -> timeout, not refused), the reachability circuit opens, and the entry flaps forever:

register -> 3 probe timeouts -> circuit open ("timeout; cooling down") -> unreachable 3 min while still heartbeating -> sweeper evicts -> heartbeat gets 410 -> re-register with a clean reachability slate -> repeat. The log shows this exact loop cycling every ~3.5 minutes.

Note this was broken before #179 too -- the central Gateway would have created a mapping proxying to its OWN localhost:7879, i.e. pointing a sorenlaptop URL at a soren-north backend. Cross-machine Directors have likely never been reachable through this path unless the remote machine ran its own Gateway.

## Potential fixes

**Option A (recommended): the Director self-provisions its own serve mapping.**
The machine that owns the loopback listener should own the serve mapping. The Director already shells the tailscale CLI to resolve its MagicDNS name; extend that to run `tailscale serve --bg --https=<port> http://localhost:<port>` for its own allocated port at startup, re-assert it periodically (same self-healing rationale as #179 -- the serve table loses entries), and `tailscale serve --https=<port> off` on graceful shutdown. The Gateway provisioner keeps the 443 front door and the orphan sweep; its per-Director mapping duty for local Directors can stay for old builds or be retired later.
Caveats: requires tailnet HTTPS certs enabled (already true for this tailnet); serialize CLI calls; if the serve command fails, log the exact error and do NOT register the endpoint (no fallback).

**Option B: verify-before-advertise.**
Before POSTing /directors/register, the Director probes its own advertised URL from the outside (GET https://[magicdns]:[port]/healthz). If unreachable it logs a precise, actionable error on the laptop ("advertised endpoint not reachable; tailscale serve mapping missing for port 7879") and does not register a dead endpoint. This does not fix reachability by itself but converts a silent 3.5-minute flap loop on the Gateway into one clear error on the machine that can act on it. Pairs well with Option A (verify after provisioning).

**Option C: Gateway-side probe-on-register + honest UI message.**
On /directors/register from a non-local machine, the Gateway immediately probes the endpoint; on failure it can either reject with 422 and a reason the Director logs, or accept but stamp the entry "never reachable since registration". The Cockpit message becomes actionable ("endpoint never answered; is tailscale serve provisioned on SORENLAPTOP?") instead of the generic "timeout; cooling down". Also worth doing for diagnosability even with Option A.

**Rejected alternative:** binding Kestrel to the Tailscale interface and advertising `http://100.x.y.z:7879` directly. It contradicts the locked design (loopback-only, HTTPS via serve, tailnet as trust boundary) and would additionally hit the Windows firewall inbound-block on the laptop.

## Immediate workaround (no code)

Run once on SORENLAPTOP (mapping persists in the serve config across reboots):

```
tailscale serve --bg --https=7879 http://localhost:7879
```

If the laptop ever runs more than one Director (ports 7880+), each port needs its own mapping -- which is exactly why Option A should own this.

## Secondary observations from the same log

- `DirectorRegistry.RecordUnreachable` logs "circuit OPEN after N failures" on EVERY failure at or above the threshold (after 8, 9, 10... failures), not only on the open transition -- log noise.
- The evict -> 410 -> re-register cycle clears the reachability slate (`Upsert` removes the `_reach` entry), so every ~3.5 min the fleet fan-out pays 3 fresh 2 s probe timeouts for a Director known to be structurally dead. Acceptable once the root cause is fixed, but worth remembering if more remote workstations join.

comments.body:
# Director Self-Serve: every Director owns its own Tailscale Serve front door

**Status:** PLAN ONLY - nothing implemented.
**Issue:** #197 (SORENLAPTOP permanently unreachable)
**Decision:** Option A chosen by Soren 2026-06-06. Reverse-tunnel (Option B) explicitly rejected for now.

---

## Problem recap (proven in #197)

There are two channels between a Director and the Gateway:

1. **Outbound announcements** (Director -> Gateway): register, heartbeat every 15 s,
   doorbell. These always work - it is why SORENLAPTOP shows up in the Cockpit at all.
2. **Inbound work** (Gateway/Cockpit -> Director): list sessions, read buffers, submit
   prompts, screenshots, kill/rename, and the Cockpit's DIRECT terminal WebSocket
   (wss://<magicdns>:<port>/sessions/{sid}/stream). The Director is dumb metal exposing
   REST; the server genuinely must reach it.

The Director binds Kestrel to **loopback only** (locked security design). The only
inbound path is a `tailscale serve --https=<port>` mapping **on the Director's own
machine**. The only component that creates such mappings is the **Gateway's**
TailscaleServeProvisioner, which since #179 (correctly) maps only LOCAL Directors.
A Workstation-role machine has no Gateway, so **no component ever opens its inbound
door**. The Director advertises `https://<magicdns>:<port>` - an endpoint that is dead
by construction - and the Gateway flaps through a register/timeout/evict/410/re-register
loop every ~3.5 minutes forever.

This is not a version issue. No build has ever provisioned serve on a Gateway-less
machine.

## Target end state (the deployment story)

On ANY new Windows machine:

1. Install Tailscale, log into the tailnet. (MagicDNS + HTTPS certs are tailnet-level
   settings and are already enabled.)
2. Install cc-director (Workstation role).
3. Set gateway.url (Settings page or config.json).

Done. The Director provisions its own serve mapping, verifies its advertised URL
actually answers, registers, and its sessions appear in the Cockpit with a live
terminal. Zero manual tailscale commands, ever. If anything is missing (no tailscale,
not logged in, serve fails), the Director surfaces ONE precise, actionable error and
does NOT register a dead endpoint. No fallbacks.

---

## Workstream 1: Director-side serve self-provisioning

New class `TailscaleServeSelfProvisioner` (location: `CcDirector.ControlApi`, next to
GatewayClient; it is part of the Director's remote-reachability concern, not the
Gateway's).

Lifecycle, wired into ControlApiHost startup after PortAllocator has assigned the
port and Kestrel is listening:

- **Assert:** run `tailscale serve --bg --https=<port> http://localhost:<port>` for the
  Director's own allocated port. Idempotent.
- **Re-assert on a timer (~5 min):** the serve table is known to lose entries with no
  cc-director process removing them (#179 evidence: the 443 front door vanished in
  production). Same self-healing rationale as the Gateway's reconcile.
- **Graceful shutdown:** `tailscale serve --https=<port> off` for the own port only.
  Crash recovery needs nothing special: ports are stable per slot (PortAllocator), so
  the next startup re-asserts the same mapping. A crashed Director's leftover mapping
  proxies to a dead loopback port until then - harmless (connection refused locally,
  Gateway circuit opens) and self-corrects on restart. The Gateway's existing orphan
  sweep does NOT run on remote machines; accept the bounded leak (max the 7879-7898
  range) rather than build a second sweeper. Document this.
- **Failure = loud stop, not fallback:** if tailscale.exe is absent, the daemon is not
  running, the node is logged out, or the serve command errors: log the exact CLI
  output, expose the failure on the Director's status (see Workstream 5), and make
  GatewayClient SKIP registration (extend the existing "no tailnet endpoint to
  advertise; staying local-only" path with the specific reason). The Director keeps
  working locally - that is the truthful state.

Implementation notes:

- Reuse the RunTailscale pattern from the Gateway provisioner (15 s process timeout,
  stdout+stderr capture). Extract the shared CLI runner into CcDirector.Core (e.g.
  `Core/Network/TailscaleCli.cs`) so Gateway and Director use one implementation; keep
  a test seam (Func delegate) exactly like `GatewayClient.MagicDnsResolver`.
- The serve mapping must exist BEFORE the first /directors/register POST. Order in
  ControlApiHost: bind Kestrel -> self-provision serve -> verify (Workstream 3) ->
  GatewayClient.Start().

## Workstream 2: cross-process tailscale CLI lock

`tailscale serve` is a full-config read-modify-write. After this change, on the
Gateway machine these can race: the Gateway provisioner (443 front door + sweep),
and up to 5+ local Directors each asserting their own port. Concurrent writers can
clobber each other's mappings - this is plausibly the actual mechanism behind #179's
"entries vanish with nothing removing them".

- All serve-mutating CLI calls (serve on/off; status reads can stay lock-free) go
  through ONE **named OS mutex** (e.g. `Global\cc-director-tailscale-serve`) shared
  across all cc-director processes on the machine. Lives in the shared TailscaleCli
  class from Workstream 1.
- Both the Gateway provisioner and the new Director self-provisioner adopt it.
- Mutex hold time is bounded by the existing 15 s CLI timeout; take the mutex with a
  timeout (e.g. 30 s) and treat failure-to-acquire as a failed CLI call (log, retry on
  the next reconcile tick).

## Workstream 3: verify-before-advertise

After asserting the mapping, the Director probes its OWN advertised URL from the
outside-in: `GET https://<magicdns>:<port>/healthz` (real HTTPS through the serve
front door, not loopback).

- Retry with backoff for up to ~60 s: the FIRST serve on a node can trigger Lets
  Encrypt cert issuance, which takes seconds.
- Only when the probe answers does GatewayClient register. The "register a lie, flap
  forever" failure mode becomes impossible.
- On verify failure after retries: same loud-stop path as Workstream 1 (precise error
  on the machine that can act on it, no registration).
- Re-verify opportunistically on the re-assert timer; if verification regresses,
  unregister is NOT needed (the Gateway's circuit/evict already handles a
  went-dark endpoint) but log the regression locally.

## Workstream 4: shrink the Gateway provisioner to one owner per mapping

- Directors own their own port mappings (new builds). The Gateway provisioner keeps:
  the 443 front door, and the orphan sweep / per-Director mapping for LOCAL Directors
  as a back-compat layer for old-build Directors during transition.
- The sweep must NOT remove a mapping a live local new-build Director just asserted -
  current logic already keys on "live local Director ports", which stays correct since
  desired state is identical whether the Gateway or the Director asserted it. Verify
  with a test.
- Once the whole fleet is on self-serving builds, optionally retire the Gateway's
  per-Director mapping duty (follow-up, not this change).

## Workstream 5: honest diagnostics end to end

- **Director side:** Director status (served HTML + /healthz payload + Settings) shows
  remote reachability state: OK (verified <when>) / FAILED (<exact reason: no
  tailscale.exe | logged out | serve error text | verify timeout>).
- **Gateway/Cockpit side:** replace the generic "unreachable (timeout; cooling down)"
  with actionable truth. Distinguish two cases in the machineErrors envelope:
  - never answered since registration -> "SORENLAPTOP registered but its endpoint never
    answered - check Tailscale Serve / Director log on that machine"
  - was reachable, went dark -> current timeout wording is fine.
  The registry already has FirstUnreachableAt and RecordReachable; add a
  "was ever reachable" bit per entry.
- **Log-noise fix (found in #197):** DirectorRegistry.RecordUnreachable logs
  "circuit OPEN after N failures" on EVERY failure >= threshold; log only on the
  closed->open transition.

## Workstream 6: installer / setup preflight

- Workstation-role setup (and the Settings gateway-connect flow) gains a Tailscale
  preflight: tailscale.exe present -> daemon running -> logged in -> MagicDNS name
  resolves. Each failure produces the exact remediation step (winget install
  tailscale.Tailscale / tailscale up / login). Detection only - the installer does not
  install Tailscale itself (explicit user action, keep the installer honest).
- Docs: docs/public deployment page gets the 3-step story (Tailscale -> cc-director ->
  gateway.url) and the troubleshooting table keyed off the Workstream 5 statuses.

## Workstream 7: tests + rollout

Tests:
- Unit: self-provisioner lifecycle via the CLI seam (assert on start, re-assert on
  timer, off on shutdown, loud-stop on CLI failure); GatewayClient skips registration
  until verify passes; registry "was ever reachable" wording; mutex serialization
  (two fake callers, interleaving).
- Integration (manual/QA): the laptop is the real-world fixture. Acceptance = the
  target end state above, plus: kill tailscale on the laptop -> Director logs the
  precise error and stops registering; restart tailscale -> self-heals within one
  reconcile tick.

Rollout:
1. Land behind normal release (no flag needed - on machines where the Gateway already
   maps local Directors the command is idempotent and now mutex-serialized).
2. Release vNext; laptop updates via auto-update (or fresh install - we have access).
3. Verify acceptance on SORENLAPTOP; then remove the manual serve mapping if one was
   added as an interim workaround, and watch one full day of Gateway logs for flap
   loops (there should be none).
4. Follow-up issue: retire Gateway per-Director mapping once fleet is current.

## Explicitly rejected

- **Reverse tunnel (Option B):** all traffic multiplexed over one outbound WebSocket.
  Architecturally clean ("pure ping-in") but re-plumbs every fan-out call AND the
  direct Cockpit->Director terminal stream through a Gateway relay - a large rework
  that discards what Tailscale already provides. Documented as the long-term option
  if non-tailnet machines ever must join the fleet.
- **Binding Kestrel to the Tailscale interface / advertising http://100.x.y.z:port:**
  violates the locked loopback-only + HTTPS-via-serve trust design and additionally
  hits the Windows inbound firewall.
- **Gateway provisioning remote machines:** impossible by mechanism - a serve mapping
  proxies to localhost on the machine that runs the CLI.

Implementation status (2026-06-06): Workstreams 1-5 and 7 are built and green locally (uncommitted).

- TailscaleCli (Core/Network): shared CLI runner; every serve-MUTATING call serialized through a cross-process named mutex. This is also the likely root-cause fix for the mysteriously clobbered serve table (#179, #200) - concurrent `tailscale serve` invocations interleave the CLI whole-table read-modify-write.
- TailscaleServeSelfProvisioner (ControlApi): the Director asserts its OWN serve mapping at startup, re-asserts every 5 min (only writes when missing), removes it on graceful shutdown. Loud-stop on failure, no fallbacks. Fixed-range ports only; ephemeral-port hosts (hosted agents, tests) excluded per the #179 lesson.
- Verify-before-advertise: GatewayClient.EndpointVerifier gates every register POST - assert mapping, then probe the advertised https://<magicdns>:<port>/healthz from the outside-in. The probe is the gate; the mapping step is best-effort-but-loud so a working gateway.tailnetEndpoint override still registers. Register-loop backoff covers first-serve TLS cert issuance.
- Gateway provisioner now uses the shared mutex-serialized CLI (behavior unchanged otherwise).
- Registry tracks was-ever-reachable (survives the evict/410/re-register cycle); the Cockpit message for a never-answered endpoint is now actionable: "endpoint never answered since registration (...) - check Tailscale Serve / the Director log on (machine)". Circuit-open logging throttled to the transition + every 10th failure.
- Tests: 4 new test classes; Gateway.Tests 259 and Core.Tests 1535 pass (only known pre-existing parallel-run flakes, green in isolation). Full solution builds clean.

Remaining: WS6 (installer Tailscale preflight + public docs), release, deploy to SORENLAPTOP, live acceptance (including the kill-tailscale -> precise error -> restart -> self-heal pass).

## Live recurrence + manual resolution on SORENLAPTOP (2026-06-06) - and the precise remaining gap

The exact failure this issue describes hit again today, and the diagnosis confirms the implementation status:

**Status check: the self-provisioner IS implemented but has NEVER shipped.**

- `TailscaleServeSelfProvisioner` landed on main in 457d00b (15 files, live-verified on a slot-5 dev Director: startup assert, 5-min self-heal, graceful shutdown removal).
- `git merge-base --is-ancestor 457d00b e6e9388` = NO: the commit is NOT in v0.6.8 (e6e9388), and is in no release tag.
- SORENLAPTOP runs release v0.6.8 -> it never had the code -> no serve mapping -> Gateway probes timed out while heartbeats kept landing (the one-way signature this issue documents).

**Incident timeline (today):**

- ~16:38 Cockpit shows SORENLAPTOP UNREACHABLE: "endpoint never answered since registration (timeout)".
- Diagnosed from the gateway machine: tailnet healthy (direct, 92ms), TCP to <tailnet-ip>:7879 times out. Firewall RULED OUT: with a serve mapping active, tailscaled answers TCP in userspace before the Windows firewall is consulted - timeout therefore means mapping absent, full stop.
- An agent ON the laptop applied the documented workaround: `tailscale serve --bg --https=7879 http://localhost:7879`, then verified: mapping present in serve status, `https://sorenlaptop.taildb08ed.ts.net:7879/healthz` returns 200 end-to-end, and the "heartbeat 410 -> re-register" churn in the Director log STOPPED (~100s of clean heartbeats after the last 410 at 16:53:19).
- Cockpit confirmed the Director reachable with sessions visible again.

**Remaining work to actually close this issue:**

1. Ship a release containing 457d00b.
2. Update SORENLAPTOP to it.
3. Live acceptance on the laptop (the kill/restart-tailscale self-heal test deferred from the slot-5 verification) - at which point today's hand-made mapping becomes redundant (the provisioner re-asserts the same mapping; no conflict expected, but verify it adopts/overwrites cleanly).

**Tied issues filed from today's incident:**

- #223 - automatic two-way connectivity self-test after install/startup + troubleshooting popup: would have named this failure in seconds instead of requiring manual cross-machine archaeology. Its version-check rung exists precisely for today's root cause ("build predates #197").
- #224 - always-visible two-way indicator (large green check) + disconnected banner with agent-ready fix prompt: heartbeats stayed green through this entire outage, which is why the indicator must key on the two-way handshake and never on heartbeat success.

Closing: live acceptance on SORENLAPTOP complete.

- Laptop auto-updated 0.6.8 -> 0.6.9 (release with the TailscaleServeSelfProvisioner, 457d00b).
- On startup it asserted its own serve mapping - zero manual provisioning. First time this machine was reachable cross-machine: GET https://soren.taildb08ed.ts.net:7879/healthz answered 200 from the gateway machine.
- The remaining blocker (no gateway.url configured) was named in plain text by the #223 troubleshooter report instead of requiring manual probing.
- After setting the Gateway URL in Settings (no restart), the laptop registered and passed the #224 two-way handshake: twoWayVerifiedAt=2026-06-07T14:56:06Z stamped on the registration, green check on the laptop's screen.

The diagnostic ladder that this incident had to be walked by hand is now automated (#223/#224, shipped in v0.6.9).

title: Cross-machine Director (SORENLAPTOP) permanently unreachable: nothing provisions tailscale serve on workstation-only machines

body:
## Symptom

In the Cockpit triage rail, a session can show a RED dot + "WaitingForInput" while simultaneously showing the yellow "wingman reading..." rail line and header chip. Screenshot showed session "websites - ai knowledgebase" red, sitting in the NEEDS YOU bucket, with "wingman reading..." under it.

This is the same class of bug as #192 (yellow-while-briefing), regressed for the Gateway/Cockpit path by the #187 architecture move.

## Root cause

Two different owners write two halves of the state, and the Cockpit renders both without reconciling them:

1. `StatusColor` is stamped by the DIRECTOR (`SessionStatusWingman.ColorFor`). Since #187 deleted the Director-side brief pipeline, the Director does not know the Gateway wingman is briefing, so at turn end it stamps red.
2. `BriefingState` is stamped by the GATEWAY brief agent (`GatewayEndpoints.cs` ~line 315, "the one writer now").
3. The Cockpit renders the dot/border from `StatusColor` (`SessionRail.razor` lines 35/38) but the rail line from `BriefingState` (line 64). Result: red dot + "wingman reading..." at the same time.
4. Triage bucketing (`SessionOrdering.Classify`, `SessionOrdering.cs:37-40`) uses ONLY `StatusColor == "red"`, so a mid-brief session is classified NEEDS YOU, then may flop to ACTIVE when the brief lands (or get refuted by assessedState). Sessions visibly bounce between triage groups during every brief.

The desktop fix for #192 lived in the Director's ColorFor, which no longer sees briefing state after #187. The aggregated Gateway view has no equivalent.

## Invariant (the rule)

While the wingman is reading (`BriefingState == "Briefing"`), the session IS yellow - dot, border, header chip, and triage bucket must all agree. Red ("needs you") may only appear AFTER the brief lands. One state at a time, never a blend.

## Proposed fix

Derive one EFFECTIVE color in shared contract code so every consumer agrees:

- Add e.g. `SessionOrdering.EffectiveColor(SessionDto s)`: if `BriefingState == "Briefing"` return yellow, else `StatusColor`.
- `SessionOrdering.Classify` uses the effective color, so a briefing session classifies as ACTIVE (not NEEDS YOU) until the brief lands. (OnHold still wins.)
- `SessionRail.razor` dot/border + `Cockpit.razor` header dot/badge render the effective color.
- Unit tests in `SessionOrderingTests`: briefing + red raw -> yellow + Active bucket; brief landed (`BriefingState != "Briefing"`) + red -> red + NeedsYou.

This keeps the two-owner model (#186/#187) intact - nobody rewrites the Director's rawState; the view layer just folds the Gateway's briefing stamp into one coherent presentation, in one shared place.
title: Cockpit: red dot + 'wingman reading...' shown at once - briefing must render yellow everywhere incl. triage bucket

body: the closed session from the cockpit is not working correctly it only closes down call code terminal it needs to kill also the session you can see sitting in the left side so we need to look at seeing what's going on with the rest api

comments.body: Root cause found and written up in #202: the Director-side DELETE /sessions/{sid} correctly kills claude.exe and removes the session from the SessionManager (since v0.6.7), but the desktop window never subscribes to SessionManager.OnSessionRemoved, so the rail row on the left stays as a zombie. Fix plan + verification plan are in #202.

Root cause was #202 (MainWindow never subscribed to OnSessionRemoved, so REST kills left a zombie rail row wrapping the dead session). Fixed in 28ca747 and verified live: kill via DELETE /sessions/{sid} now removes the row from the desktop rail immediately.

title: Kill session not working

body:
## Symptom

While the wingman is reading a just-finished turn (BriefingState=Briefing), the Cockpit shows the session as RED everywhere:

- Left rail (Triage): the row sits in the NEEDS YOU bucket with a red dot + red border, with the amber "wingman reading..." rail-line under it - contradictory.
- Detail header: red dot + WAITINGFORINPUT, next to the amber "wingman reading..." chip - same contradiction in one row of pixels.

Seen live 2026-06-06 on session "mindzieWeb - AI - QA" (SOREN_NORTH / director 1710e5fb): the turn had just completed, the wingman had not produced the brief yet, and the session was already counted in "5 need you" and painted red.

## Expected

Per the turn-briefing architecture (docs/architecture/wingman/TURN_BRIEFING.md) and the desktop behavior: turn end -> YELLOW briefing window while the wingman reads -> only then red (with the brief's railLine) or back to green/idle. While yellow, the session must not be counted or painted as "needs you" - the whole point of the brief is that we do not yet KNOW whether it needs you.

The code already documents this contract:

- `src/CcDirector.Core/Wingman/StatusColor.cs:13` - "yellow = the Wingman is reading the screen and narrating a briefing."
- `src/CcDirector.Core/Sessions/BriefingState.cs:7` - "Briefing -> yellow 'briefing...'; detector-red + Briefed -> red with the brief's railLine."

## Root cause

The TurnBriefOrchestrator (`src/CcDirector.Core/Wingman/TurnBriefOrchestrator.cs`) sets `BriefingState=Briefing` but never flips `StatusColor`; the activity detector's red survives through the briefing window. The Cockpit then renders StatusColor verbatim:

- `SessionRail.razor` - dot/border = DotColor(s.StatusColor) (red), even when s.BriefingState == "Briefing" (it already special-cases the rail-line text, so the state is available on the DTO).
- `SessionOrdering` - the Triage NEEDS YOU bucket keys on StatusColor == "red", so briefing sessions land in NEEDS YOU.
- The "N need you" header counter has the same problem.
- Detail header dot + state label: same.

(The desktop got yellow from the old per-session WingmanService writing StatusColor=yellow directly; sessions on the new turn-brief pipeline only get BriefingState.)

## Fix direction (decide during implementation)

Either:
1. Director-side: TurnBriefOrchestrator writes StatusColor=yellow for the duration of the briefing window (restoring the old WingmanService semantics), so every client renders it correctly for free; or
2. Cockpit-side: treat BriefingState=Briefing as yellow presentation (dot, border, bucket, counter) overriding the red.

Option 1 matches the "StatusColor is Director-authored, never derived" rule the Cockpit already follows, and keeps phone/desktop/web consistent. Note the rawState/assessedState two-owner model from the Gateway Wingman architecture (#173) is the long-term home for this distinction.

## Acceptance

- While a session's wingman is reading: yellow dot/border, NOT in the NEEDS YOU bucket, not in the "N need you" count, header shows the briefing state.
- When the brief lands and says needs-you: red with the railLine (existing behavior).
- When the brief lands and says nothing needed: back to idle color, no red flash.
title: Cockpit shows RED 'needs you' while the wingman is still reading the turn - should be the YELLOW briefing state

body:
## What

Each session row in the Cockpit's left rail (Tree and Triage views) needs a three-dot menu, mirroring the desktop Director's session context menu. Today the rail rows are plain click-to-select buttons; the only session actions live in the detail header (Rename / Kill / Handover), so common actions like closing a session or filing a GitHub issue require selecting the session first and hunting the header.

## Spec: mirror the desktop menu

The desktop context menu is built in `src/CcDirector.Avalonia/MainWindow.axaml.cs` (ShowSessionContextMenu, ~line 748). Items, in order, with the web adaptation for each:

| Desktop item | Web adaptation |
|---|---|
| Rename | Reuse the existing rename flow (PATCH /sessions/{sid}); inline or small dialog |
| Relink Session... | Calls the Director relink endpoint; needs the transcript-picker UI (GET /claude-transcripts) |
| Copy Handover Info | Same text block (session name + id + Director identity/version) to the browser clipboard |
| Open .jsonl in Explorer | Machine-local on desktop. Web equivalent: open the transcript in the Cockpit transcript viewer page (new tab) |
| Open in Explorer | Machine-local; no direct web equivalent. Decide: omit, or show the repo path with a copy button |
| Open in VS Code | Machine-local; decide: omit, or vscode:// URL (only works when browsing on the Director machine) |
| New GitHub Issue | Derive the repo's origin remote (same as desktop OpenNewGitHubIssue) and open GitHub's new-issue form in a new tab. Works naturally on the web since it is just a URL |
| Hold / Take Off Hold | POST /sessions/{sid}/hold already exists |
| Add Wingman / Remove Wingman | Needs a Director endpoint check; desktop toggles Session.WingmanEnabled |
| Close Session | DELETE /sessions/{sid} (the detail header Kill already uses it); keep the same confirm/arm pattern |

Keep the desktop's separator grouping: (Rename/Relink/Copy) | (.jsonl) | (Explorer/VS Code/New GitHub Issue) | (Hold/Wingman) | (Close).

## Notes

- The menu must work in BOTH rail views (tree + triage) since they share the row template (`SessionRail.razor` SessionRow fragment).
- The three-dot button must not steal the row's click-to-select; standard stopPropagation.
- Machine-local items (Explorer / VS Code) are the only ones without a clean web equivalent - everything else maps to existing Director endpoints or plain URLs. Decide omit-vs-adapt per item during implementation; the GitHub issue item must NOT be dropped (it is just a URL and is one of the main motivations).

comments.body:
Shipped the first cut in bf3ee69 and hot-deployed to the live Cockpit:

Implemented: Rename, Copy Handover Info, New GitHub Issue (new GET /sessions/{sid}/github-urls on the Director; old Directors show a clear relaunch message), Hold/Take Off Hold, Add/Remove Wingman, Close Session (two-step confirm). Works in both tree and triage views.

Still open from the desktop menu: Relink Session (needs the transcript-picker UI), Open .jsonl (could map to the Cockpit transcript viewer), Open in Explorer / VS Code (machine-local - likely omit). Leaving this issue open for those.

Implemented in commit bf3ee69. Per-session kebab menu in the Cockpit rail (Rename, Copy Handover Info, New GitHub Issue, Hold/Take Off Hold, Add/Remove Wingman, two-step Close, Start bug session), stopPropagation on the row, shared SessionRow template. The required New GitHub Issue item is present; the spec's optional Relink / .jsonl-viewer / Explorer entries were marked decide-during-impl and omitted. Verified against code. Closing.

title: Cockpit: per-session three-dot menu in the left rail (mirror the desktop session context menu)

body:
## Summary

The dictation cleanup pass (`CleanupOrchestrator`, gpt-4.1-nano) can replace the user's dictated words with completely different text, and nothing in the pipeline prevents or detects it. Two findings, both log-proven:

1. **Few-shot leak (incident 2026-06-06):** the model returned one of the hardcoded few-shot examples from `CleanupOrchestrator.cs` verbatim instead of the user's actual 1,389-character dictation. The fabricated prompt was auto-submitted to a Claude session.
2. **Systemic contract violations (audit):** replaying all 207 logged cleanups against deterministic dictionary application shows the model changed text beyond its dictionary-only mandate in **35 of 207 dictations (17%)** - including wholesale paraphrasing, refusals, truncation, and meaning-flipping word swaps.

## Finding 1: Few-shot example injected as the user's prompt

On 2026-06-06 at 06:47 the user dictated a ~1,389-character prompt via the desktop SpeakDialog. The text that landed in the session was:

> yeah just push it to cc-director when you get a sec

That string is not a transcription. It is few-shot example #2 hardcoded in `src/CcDirector.Core/Dictation/CleanupOrchestrator.cs` (FewShotExamples, ~line 223):

```csharp
("yeah just push it to See Director when you get a sec",
 "yeah just push it to cc-director when you get a sec"),
```

Evidence chain:

- `director-2026-06-06-45452.log` 06:47:38:
  - `[CleanupOrchestrator] CleanAsync: profile=default, model=gpt-4.1-nano, len=1389`
  - `[DictationSession] StopAsync done: raw_len=1389, cleaned_len=51, applied=True`
- The few-shot string is exactly 51 characters.
- Screenshot at 06:49:38 shows that string as the submitted prompt and the agent responding with total confusion.

The few-shots are sent as fake user/assistant conversation turns (`CallOpenAiAsync`). Small models sometimes collapse onto a prior assistant turn and replay it - especially when the real transcript shares trigger words with an example ("cc-director", "push", "issue"). The 1,389-character dictation was discarded entirely and is unrecoverable (see "audit gap" below).

Bitter irony: the few-shots were added in 19ee313 (2026-05-31) to STOP the model from inventing text. The fix introduced the exact sentence that got injected.

## Finding 2: 17% of cleanups change the user's words beyond the dictionary

The contract (system prompt + docs): replace listed mistranscriptions with canonical terms, fix capitalization of listed vocabulary, change NOTHING else - never reword, never add/delete, never answer.

Audit method: for every `CleanupApplied=true` record in `%LOCALAPPDATA%/cc-director/dictation/sessions/*.jsonl`, apply the `dictionary.yaml` mappings to the raw transcript deterministically and diff against the model's output.

| Outcome | Count |
|---|---|
| Returned verbatim | 163 |
| Changes fully explained by dictionary | 9 |
| Changed beyond mandate (VIOLATION) | 35 (17%) |

Of the 44 records where the model changed anything, 80% of changes were unauthorized. Violation classes, with real examples from the logs:

**Wholesale replacement / answering / refusing (~14 cases)**
- raw 1,174 chars -> `"I understand."` (2026-05-28)
- `"Please create a nice table for me showing the GDP for Europe versus the US in 2025."` -> `"I'm sorry, but I can't provide a table with GDP projections..."` (the cleanup model refused the dictation)
- Multiple full paraphrases: `"You want to update the terminology in the system so that..."`
- Narration: `"I corrected the transcript by replacing all instances..."` (3x on 2026-06-01, AFTER both prompt fixes)

**Meaning-flipping word swaps - forbidden near-miss guessing (~10 cases)**
- `"a question that Claude said"` -> `"a question that cc-director said"` (2026-06-04, changed which system was meant)
- `"all of our conformance enrichments"` -> `"all of our CenCon enrichments"` (ordinary word -> product name)
- `"its own Claude code instance"` -> `"its own mindzie code instance"`
- `"sessions in director"` -> `"sessions in ConCon"` (invented a term not in the dictionary)
- `"the PTY"` -> `"the ConPTY"` (different things)

**Truncation (1 case)**
- 2026-05-26: dropped the back half of a dictation including the instruction `"So can you rewrite this message with not my words..."`

**Silent grammar/word edits (~10 cases)**
- removed `"like"`; `"You asking"` -> `"You're asking"`; `"more clear"` -> `"more clearly"`; `"Soren"` -> `"Soren Frederiksen"`; `"just start the Android emulator"` -> `"just start of the Android emulator"` (inserted a word that broke the sentence)

Timeline showing prompt engineering has failed twice:

| Date | Event |
|---|---|
| 05-21 | dictation v1 ships with LLM cleanup (dcbb663) |
| 05-24/25 | model answers/paraphrases dictations |
| 05-26 | fix 9dcad80: dictionary-only verbatim rules |
| 05-28 | still failing: 1,174 chars -> "I understand." |
| 05-31 | fix 19ee313: few-shot examples added |
| 06-01 | still failing: narration x3 |
| 06-06 | NEW failure mode: few-shot example itself leaks |

## Root cause

`CleanupOrchestrator.CleanAsync` (~lines 99-107) accepts ANY non-empty model output as the cleaned transcript. The only check is `IsNullOrWhiteSpace`. The verbatim guarantee exists only as prompt text - i.e., the model's goodwill. There is no runtime validation that the output resembles the input.

`CleanupOrchestratorLiveTests` (7 facts: verbatim echo, no-narration, term-only correction) are manual, key-gated spot checks - the model passes them and still violates ~1 in 6 real dictations.

## Aggravating factors

1. **No human gate before submit.** Desktop SpeakDialog Send (`SpeakDialog.axaml.cs` ~485-509) and the cockpit dialog's Send-while-recording submit the CLEANED text sight-unseen - the live transcript the user watched was the raw partials; the corrupted cleaned text only exists after Send is pressed. `dictate-client.js` documents "No 'Use it' confirmation step" by design.
2. **Desktop path has no audit trail.** Only `DictationEndpoint` writes the JSONL session records with full raw/cleaned text. The desktop in-process path logs lengths only, so the 06-06 incident's raw text is gone forever and desktop violations are invisible to any audit.

## Recommendations (ranked by how completely they close the hole)

1. **Replace the LLM pass with deterministic dictionary replacement.** The contract (exact wrong-form list -> canonical term, vocab capitalization) is a regex find-and-replace; the audit script above implements it deterministically. As specified, the LLM is forbidden from doing the one thing it adds over regex (fuzzy near-miss matching), so it currently contributes only risk.
2. **If the LLM stays: output validation gate in `CleanAsync`.** Reject any output not explained by dictionary edits (length-ratio + token-overlap vs raw, plus exact-match check against the few-shot strings) and fall back to raw - fail-open to raw is already the documented contract for errors.
3. **Both:** deterministic replacement always; LLM only as an optional suggester whose output must pass the gate.

Plus, regardless of choice:
- Write JSONL session records from the desktop in-process path so the next incident keeps its evidence.
- Consider surfacing cleaned-vs-raw divergence in the dialog before Send when they differ materially.

## Verification of any fix

- Replay the 35 violating records (raw transcripts are in the JSONL logs) through the fixed path: every output must be byte-identical to deterministic dictionary application of the raw text.
- Regression test: feed the few-shot user-side strings and trigger-word-laden long transcripts; output must never match a few-shot assistant string unless the input did.

comments.body:
﻿SECOND OCCURRENCE, 25 minutes after filing this issue.

2026-06-06 07:11:20, director-2026-06-06-45452.log:

```
[CleanupOrchestrator] CleanAsync: profile=default, model=gpt-4.1-nano, len=814
[DictationSession] StopAsync done: raw_len=814, cleaned_len=68, applied=True
```

The user dictated 814 characters. The text that arrived in the session was:

> Can you explain what this function does and then refactor it for me?

That is FewShotExamples[0] from CleanupOrchestrator.cs - exactly 68 characters, matching cleaned_len=68. The first incident (06:47) leaked example #2 (1389 -> 51 chars); this one leaked example #1 (814 -> 68 chars). Both via the desktop in-process path, so both raw transcripts are unrecoverable (no JSONL on that path - see "audit gap" above).

Two different hardcoded examples replacing real dictations within 25 minutes upgrades this from "intermittent" to "actively recurring". Note also 06:54:32 the same morning: raw_len=428 -> cleaned_len=281 (unexplained 35% shrink, text not recoverable).

Possibly relevant: the connect-retry work in #189 documents an OpenAI degradation blip this same morning ("the 2026-06-06 OpenAI 504 blip"). Model-side instability may raise the leak rate, but the vulnerability is ours: CleanAsync accepts any non-empty output.

﻿FIXED in 41d82c4.

The transcript no longer round-trips through the cleanup model. The model returns a JSON edit document (find/replace proposals); the new deterministic `TranscriptEditEngine` validates each edit (find must exist verbatim in the raw transcript, replace must be a canonical dictionary term, plausible-mishearing gate blocks meaning flips) and applies the survivors to the RAW text with boundary-aware replacement. Any non-conforming model output - prose, refusals, narration, leaked few-shot examples - ships the raw transcript untouched.

What this closes, point by point:

- Few-shot leakage (both 2026-06-06 incidents): structurally impossible. Few-shots are now edit documents; a leaked example proposes a find that does not exist in the real transcript and is rejected. Regression tests replay both leaked outputs plus the historical "I understand.", the GDP refusal, and the narration outputs - the transcript survives byte-for-byte in every case.
- 17% silent-alteration rate: any change not explainable as a validated dictionary edit can no longer occur. The logged meaning flips ("Claude" -> cc-director, "conformance" -> CenCon) are pinned by tests as blocked.
- Audit gap: the desktop SpeakService path now writes the same JSONL session records as the /dictate endpoint (new `Source` field distinguishes them), so raw text is never lost again.

Worst-case failure flips polarity: from "user's words replaced by garbage" to "a term left uncorrected".

Verification: 61/61 tests pass, including 7 live tests against production gpt-4.1-nano (run twice) asserting byte-identical output for term-free transcripts and exact term-only correction otherwise.

The pattern is documented for porting to other LLM-cleanup surfaces: docs/architecture/dictation/EDIT_DOCUMENT_CLEANUP.md ("LLM proposes, code disposes").

title: Dictation cleanup model replaces dictated text: few-shot example injected as user prompt; 17% of cleanups alter words beyond the dictionary

body:
## What happened

On 2026-06-06 ~06:38, OpenAI's realtime endpoint (wss://api.openai.com/v1/realtime?intent=transcription) had a ~90s blip where its edge returned HTTP 504 instead of the WebSocket 101 upgrade. Two consecutive dictation attempts failed:

```
06:37:46.856 [OpenAiRealtimeProvider] StartAsync: endpoint=wss://api.openai.com/v1/realtime?intent=transcription, model=gpt-4o-transcribe
06:38:02.153 [DictationPipeline] StartAsync: session connect FAILED: The server returned status code '504' when status code '101' was expected.
06:38:02.167 [SpeakDialog] StartAsync FAILED: The server returned status code '504' when status code '101' was expected.
```

The outage was OpenAI-side and self-resolved (connections at 06:39:07 and 06:39:26 succeeded in ~0.3s). The bug here is how the Dictate dialog behaves while the connect is failing.

## Problems with the current failure mode

1. **~15 seconds of dead air before the error.** ClientWebSocket.ConnectAsync is given no explicit timeout, so a failing upgrade hangs until the server/edge gives up (~15s observed). During that window the dialog gives no indication it is still connecting.

2. **The dialog looks like it is recording when it is not.** The elapsed timer starts immediately and keeps counting even after the ERROR state is shown (screenshot showed ERROR with the timer at 0:28.7 still running). The user keeps talking into a dead session.

3. **No automatic retry.** A single transient 504 surfaces straight to the user. The user manually retried 8 seconds later and hit the same blip; one automatic retry with a short backoff would very likely have succeeded shortly after.

4. **Audio captured during the connect window is dropped.** The capture-first pipeline correctly primes audio while connecting, but on connect failure the dialog returns "no text" and the primed audio is discarded.

## Proposed friendlier failure mode

- Explicit connect timeout (e.g. 10s) on the realtime WebSocket connect so a dead edge fails fast.
- One automatic retry with short backoff before surfacing an error; primed audio is preserved across the retry (capture keeps running), so no speech is lost if the retry succeeds.
- Dialog state machine distinguishes Connecting from Recording: show "Connecting..." until the session is live, only start the elapsed timer once connected, and stop the timer when entering ERROR.
- Error text should say what failed and what to do (transient service error, try again).

## Evidence

Log: %LOCALAPPDATA%\cc-director\logs\director\director-2026-06-06-45452.log (06:37:46 - 06:39:34)

comments.body:
Implemented (uncommitted, pending review):

**Provider (CcDirector.Core/Dictation/Providers/OpenAiRealtimeProvider.cs)**
- Per-attempt connect timeout of 10s (ClientWebSocket.ConnectAsync was previously unbounded; the 504 blip hung ~15s per attempt).
- One automatic retry with 1s backoff before surfacing the failure. The capture-first pipeline keeps buffering mic audio while StartAsync is in flight, so a successful retry loses zero speech.
- Exhausted retries throw the new DictationConnectException with a human-readable message ("Could not connect to the transcription service (2 attempts). This is usually a temporary service or network problem - wait a moment and try again. Last error: ...").
- Caller cancellation propagates immediately - never retried, never rewrapped.
- Applies to BOTH the desktop SpeakDialog and the web /dictate endpoint (DictationEndpoint.cs) since both construct this provider.

**Dialog (CcDirector.Avalonia/Voice/SpeakDialog.axaml(.cs))**
- New Connecting stage: opens with "CONNECTING" (yellow) instead of "RECORDING"; Send/Insert/Pause and the mic selector stay disabled until the session is live. Cancel always available. The timer ticks during Connecting because the mic IS capturing (capture-first) and that audio is transcribed.
- New Failed stage: the timer freezes on error (previously it kept counting next to the ERROR label - the 0:28.7 in the screenshot), bars park gray, Cancel becomes Close.
- Bonus bug fixed: a failed Resume used to write the error message INTO the transcript box while staying in the Paused stage - and in Paused, the box content is exactly what Send submits, so Send would have sent the error text as the prompt. Resume failure now keeps the user's text, surfaces the error in the status row, and leaves Resume/Send/Insert usable.

**Tests** - new OpenAiRealtimeProviderConnectTests (5 tests, offline, deterministic) drive the connect path against an in-process fake server on loopback that can answer the upgrade with HTTP 504 (exact reproduction of the outage), accept a real RFC 6455 handshake, or hang silently:
- 504 on every attempt -> DictationConnectException after exactly 2 attempts, WebSocketException inner, message contains 504 + "try again"
- 504 then accept -> recovers on the retry
- server never responds -> bounded per-attempt timeout, TimeoutException inner, fails fast (<5s with test knobs) instead of hanging
- caller cancels -> OperationCanceledException, exactly 1 attempt
- after an exhausted failure, StartAsync can be called again (session state reset)

All 95 dictation tests pass (Release).

Fixed in 33e5ab6 - bounded connect (10s/attempt) + one automatic retry in OpenAiRealtimeProvider, DictationConnectException with a human-readable message, and SpeakDialog Connecting/Failed stages (timer no longer counts in ERROR state). 95/95 dictation tests pass including 5 new offline connect-policy tests.

title: Dictate dialog: unfriendly failure mode when OpenAI realtime connect fails (504)

body:
End state of the Gateway Wingman architecture (#173): the Director is dumb metal. Once
the Gateway Turn Brief agent is live and proven, DELETE the Director-side pipeline:

- TurnBriefOrchestrator / TurnBriefGenerator side-claude --print path
- TurnBriefStore 50-ring at %LOCALAPPDATA%\cc-director\turn-briefs
- CC_TURNBRIEFS gating and the per-Director brief endpoints that the Cockpit stops using
  (consumers read briefs from the Gateway instead)

Do NOT patch the Director pipeline in the meantime - it is end-of-life.

Depends on: the Gateway Turn Brief agent running in production.
Refs: #173, docs/architecture/wingman/TURN_BRIEFING.md

comments.body:
Shipped in eca1c36 (net -869 lines) and live on the production gateway (2026-06-06). The Gateway Wingman end state (#173) is complete: #184 warm brain -> #185 stamping machine -> #186 doorbell push -> #187 the Director is dumb metal.

## Deleted
- TurnBriefOrchestrator + its ControlApiHost wiring - Directors spawn NO side claude --print for briefs anymore.
- WingmanTurnBriefGenerator (the cold-spawn path). ITurnBriefGenerator + StubTurnBriefGenerator survive (the Gateway agent's degrade tier); TurnBriefContract is the one living home of the frozen v2.3 prompt/validation.
- TurnBriefStore (the 50-ring) + CcStorage.TurnBriefs() + the Director's /sessions/{sid}/turnbriefs* endpoints. The old on-disk ring is dead data, untouched.
- The Cockpit's Director-fallback brief reads (the #185 migration bridge) and the Director-side brief client methods.
- The orchestrator/store/generator tests; the contract + package-builder tests survive, repointed at TurnBriefContract.

## What the deletion would have silently broken - handled
- SessionDto.BriefingState (the #192 yellow "wingman reading..." chip) and RailLine were written only by the deleted orchestrator. The Gateway aggregation now stamps both from its own brief agent and store (with an in-memory latest-brief cache so the per-poll stamp never touches disk). Verified live: 9 production sessions carrying Gateway-stamped Briefed states and real rail lines minutes after deploy.
- D7 feedback moved with the store: POST /sessions/{sid}/turnbriefs/feedback now lives on the GATEWAY (labeled examples to brief-feedback/), Cockpit repointed.
- Old Directors keep their internal pipeline until they update - harmless dual generation, no compat shims, per the issue's "do not patch it" directive.
- Confirmed the desktop app consumes no TurnBrief surfaces (grep-verified) - nothing lost there.

Verification: full solution builds clean (isolated worktree of the commit), surviving brief test suites green (54 Core + 31 Gateway), production gateway redeployed on eca1c36 with healthz + front door + stamped aggregation all confirmed live.

This closes the #173 architecture chain.

title: Delete the Director-side turn-brief pipeline once the Gateway brief agent is proven

body:
Locked with Soren 2026-06-05 (supersedes the earlier pull proposal in #173):

PUSH, doorbell-style:
- The Director pushes a tiny ping at turn end - payload ONLY {directorId, sessionId,
  newState}, never turn content ("announce THAT, never WHAT"). The Gateway then pulls
  the truth (JSONL/screen) via existing Director REST. Notify-then-fetch.
- Turn-end detection on the Director is the DUMB 10s-quiet timer only (agent-agnostic,
  works for Pi etc.). The ping is a hypothesis, not a verdict.
- The Director also HEARTBEATS (liveness + per-session state/last-turn-id). The
  heartbeat doubles as the reconcile channel: lost pings are harmless because the ping
  carries no data. No retry queues/outbox in the Director. Gateway catch-up pull only
  at its own startup.

Two states, one writer each:
- rawState - owned by the Director (busy/quiet, mechanical only; nothing else writes it).
- assessedState - owned by the Gateway (the brain reads the JSONL after the ping and can
  REFUTE the quiet signal, e.g. a long silent bash command = still working). The Cockpit
  displays assessedState. The Gateway pushes it back down for the Director's local UI,
  stored as a DISPLAY ANNOTATION only - never fed into the detector, never re-pushed.
  New PTY bytes auto-invalidate any standing assessment.

Note: a registration/heartbeat layer already landed in f0730d2 (InstanceRegistration,
/directors/{id}/heartbeat) - build on it rather than alongside it.

Depends on: Gateway Turn Brief agent (the consumer of the ping).
Refs: #173, docs/architecture/wingman/TURN_BRIEFING.md

comments.body:
Shipped in be65b4a (committed first, then deployed - this morning's lesson is now the workflow) and live-verified end-to-end on the production gateway (2026-06-06).

## Built exactly as locked

**Doorbell (push, notify-then-fetch):** the Director POSTs /directors/{id}/doorbell on every session activity-state change - payload ONLY {sessionId, newState}, announce THAT never WHAT. Fire-and-forget (no retries, no outbox); not sent while unregistered. Turn-end detection stays the dumb 10s-quiet detector; the Gateway pulls the truth over existing REST exactly as the #185 agent already does. Built ON the f0730d2 registration layer: ControlApiHost wires every session's OnActivityStateChanged into GatewayClient.NotifySessionState.

**Heartbeat = reconcile channel:** the 15s heartbeat body (previously null) now snapshots every session's state. Gateway-side, one tracker (TurnEndWatcher.Observe, idempotent on repeated states) is fed by doorbell pings AND heartbeat snapshots - a lost ping heals within 15s, no Director-side queues. Compatible both directions (old Gateways ignore the body; old Directors send none).

**The 2s poll is gone.** Two pulls remain: a one-time startup catch-up sweep, and a 15s reconcile poll ONLY for Directors that have never pushed - this matters because file-discovered local Directors (no gateway.url in config.json, which is exactly soren-north's setup) and old builds would otherwise never signal. The registry marks a Director state-reporting on its first ping; the poll skips it from then on.

**Two states, one writer each:**
- rawState = SessionDto.ActivityState - Director-owned, mechanical, unchanged.
- assessedState - Gateway-owned (new SessionAssessments): derived when a brief lands (needsYou != null -> WaitingForInput; null -> Idle, the brain REFUTING a red quiet signal). Any Working observation invalidates it. Stamped on the /sessions aggregation; Cockpit (rail, session header, fleet cards) displays AssessedState ?? ActivityState; pushed down to the owning Director via new POST /sessions/{sid}/assessment as a DISPLAY ANNOTATION only - never fed into the detector, never re-pushed, wiped by any real state change (Session.SetActivityState).

## Live verification (production gateway, heterogeneous fleet)

- Test Director (new build, gateway.url set) HTTP-registered -> doorbells 200 on session create/working/turn-end, registry logged "is state-reporting; reconcile poll skips it" on the FIRST ping.
- Turn end -> doorbell -> brief stamped by the warm brain within seconds (no poll involved).
- THE refutation observed live: aggregated /sessions showed assessed=Idle while raw=WaitingForInput (brain read the turn, nothing needed); the Director's own API showed annotation=Idle (push-down worked).
- Invalidation: a new prompt flipped the session Working and the annotation went null instantly on both sides; the next turn re-briefed (turn 4) and re-assessed Idle.
- Compatibility: Soren's old 0.6.3 Directors kept getting briefed via the 15s reconcile path (the laptop's session was assessed WaitingForInput mid-test), and their 404 on the assessment push-down is logged best-effort, never fatal.

9 new unit tests (push-fed Observe semantics incl. lost-ping reconcile, assessment derivation + invalidation, OnBriefStored hook, state-reporting flag); full solution builds clean (verified in an isolated worktree of the commit before push).

Next: #187 deletes the Director-side brief pipeline + the Cockpit's Director fallback.

title: Director push: turn-end doorbell + heartbeat reconcile + rawState/assessedState two-owner model

body:
The Turn Brief Agent moves to the Gateway (locked in #173): ONE warm claude session for
the whole fleet reads each completed turn and emits a TurnBrief (frozen v2.3 contract,
TurnBriefDto), then gets /clear'ed - a stamping machine, not the wingman.

Work:
- Brief pipeline on the Gateway: turn-end trigger -> pull truth from the owning Director
  (/turns widgets + screen tail) -> assemble TurnPackage (builder is pure, reusable) ->
  warm-brain ask with the TurnBriefGenerator prompt -> validated TurnBrief JSON.
- Queue coalesces per session; watch-cancel preserved.
- Storage: append-only JSON on disk Gateway-side (no 50-ring); served to the Cockpit
  from the Gateway.
- v1 trigger may use the Gateway's existing fleet poll; the push doorbell (separate
  issue) replaces it when it lands.
- Prompt changes take effect WITHOUT touching any Director - the deployment pain this
  whole architecture removes.

Depends on: AgentBrain v2 (in-process warm brain).
Refs: #173, docs/architecture/wingman/TURN_BRIEFING.md, src/CcDirector.Core/Wingman/TurnPackage.cs

comments.body:
Built, live-verified, COMMITTED (8c1c642 + 1859f49), and stamping briefs for the production fleet on soren-north (2026-06-06).

## What was built

1. **TurnBriefContract** (Core/Wingman): the frozen v2.3 prompt + mechanical validation extracted from WingmanTurnBriefGenerator into ONE shared class - the Gateway agent and the Director path (until #187 deletes it) run the exact same contract. Prompt changes land in one place, no Director deployment anywhere.
2. **TurnEndWatcher** (Gateway/Briefing): the v1 trigger - a 2s fleet poll (circuit-breaker aware) observing each session's ActivityState across sweeps; fires on Working -> WaitingForInput/Idle, plus first-sighting backfill at Gateway boot (the agent skips already-briefed turns so it never double-briefs). One small class, ready to be replaced by the #186 push doorbell.
3. **GatewayTurnBriefAgent**: the warm-brain stamping machine - ONE queued agent for the whole fleet. Per-session coalescing, watch-cancel preserved, 2.5s settle, pulls /turns widgets + screen tail from the owning Director over existing REST (zero Director changes), builds the TurnPackage with the same pure builder, asks the #184 warm brain, /clear after EVERY brief, validates v2.3, degrades to the honest stub on failure with collapsed brain recovery. Kill switch CC_TURNBRIEFS=0.
4. **GatewayTurnBriefStore**: append-only .jsonl per session (per #173: NO 50-ring - chapters keep their opening cards forever); regeneration replaces by TurnNumber on READ, never rewrites disk.
5. **Gateway endpoints** GET /sessions/{sid}/turnbriefs(+/latest), same DTO as the Director's; **Cockpit repointed** (BriefPane + story timeline) to read Gateway-first with Director fallback until #187.
6. 22 unit tests (store/watcher/agent) + a regression test for the production find below; Gateway.Tests sets CC_TURNBRIEFS=0 assembly-wide so test-spun hosts never poll fake Directors.

## Live verification

- Isolated stack (own CC_DIRECTOR_ROOT, gateway :7981 + slot-5 Director): real turn -> watcher fired -> brain cold-spawned 3.9s -> brief in 20.5s, validated v2.3, served from the Gateway endpoint. A question-turn produced a perfect needsYou: keys/single, options Red->"1" / Blue->"2", VERBATIM evidence, blocking, railLine - and the dev Cockpit rendered all of it from the Gateway store (chapters separating the two pieces of work correctly). Evidence: .temp/qa-185/.
- **Production find, fixed in 1859f49**: a watch-cancel mid-ask fires brain recovery (by design), but BrainSupervisor.GetAsync's lock-free fast path handed the agent out MID-restart -> every next ask failed -> stub + another recovery, a restart storm. GetAsync now always takes the gate; concurrent recoveries collapse into one. Regression test included.
- Production now: gateway 0.6.7+1859f49, brief pipeline live against the real fleet - 3 real gateway-brain briefs stored, 0 stubs, including real rail lines for live working sessions.

## Rollout note

eed2777 (a shared-index stowaway commit from a parallel session) carried half these files and broke the main build; 8c1c642 landed the missing half (build verified green in a clean worktree before push). CI's remaining red on main is the pre-existing flaky trio (dictation-connect timing, Wingman prompt-injection, scheduler persistence), not this work.

Next in the chain: #186 (push doorbell replaces the TurnEndWatcher poll) -> #187 (delete the Director-side pipeline + Cockpit fallback).

title: Gateway Turn Brief agent: the warm-brain stamping machine (one queued agent, cleared per brief)

body:
The AgentBrain library (#172 follow-up) was built as a REST client driving a session on
a Director. That design was declared wrong on 2026-06-05: the brain must live INSIDE the
Gateway process - it cannot depend on a Director being up. The whole Gateway-as-tray-app
conversion (shipped v0.6.6) exists so the Gateway runs as the user and can host
claude.exe with the user's Max OAuth.

The building block already shipped in f0730d2: HostedAgent self-hosts claude.exe
in-process (no Director), with the per-CLI driver layer (IAgentDriver/ClaudeDriver).

Work:
- Rework AgentBrain's public surface (AskAsync / ClearAsync / RestartAsync / KillAsync /
  GetHealthAsync) onto HostedAgent instead of Director REST.
- Keep the three determinism rules, now local: quiet-gate sends, JSONL-as-answer-channel
  (direct file reads), relink after /clear.
- The AgentBrain.Panel becomes the test harness for the in-process brain.
- The Gateway tray app owns the brain's lifecycle (create on demand, RestartAsync as the
  recovery verb, health surfaced in the Settings window).

This is step 2 of the agreed build order (1 tray switch DONE -> 2 this -> 3 Turn Brief
agent -> 4 Director push -> 5 delete Director brief pipeline).

Refs: #172, #173, docs/plans/agent-brain.md, docs/features/hosted-agent/QA_REPORT.html

comments.body:
Built, tested, and deployed live on soren-north (2026-06-06). Most of the engine already existed from the f1daf1c hosted-agent work (HostedAgent : IAgentBrain with the three determinism rules running locally); this issue's remaining work was the transport retirement and the Gateway ownership wiring.

## What changed

1. **REST transport retired.** AgentBrainClient + AgentBrainOptions DELETED, along with the CcDirector.AgentBrain.Tests fake-HTTP project (removed from the solution). CcDirector.AgentBrain is now only the shared contract: IAgentBrain, AskResult / ClearResult / BrainHealth, AgentBrainException, BrainLog.
2. **BrainSupervisor** (new, src/CcDirector.HostedAgent/BrainSupervisor.cs): owns ONE brain for a long-lived host - create on demand (first GetAsync spawns claude.exe), RestartAsync as the recovery verb (also performs first start), graceful Ctrl+C-then-kill on dispose so the host never leaks a claude.exe. All transitions serialized through one gate. 14 unit tests on the existing fake driver/backend pair (57/57 green in CcDirector.HostedAgent.Tests).
3. **Gateway tray app owns the brain lifecycle**: GatewayTrayController creates the supervisor (workdir %LOCALAPPDATA%\cc-director\brain, logs into the gateway FileLog), disposes it on Quit and on /shutdown. The Settings window gets a BRAIN section: live state (state / idle / context tokens / session id+pid, 2s refresh) and a Restart Brain button.
4. **Panel is hosted-only**: Director (REST) mode, --mode-director, and --director are gone; the panel is the manual harness for the in-process brain.
5. **Bug found+fixed during live verification**: ClaudeSessionReader.GetProjectFolder missed that claude.exe encodes EVERY non-alphanumeric character as a dash - dots were left intact, so transcripts under any path containing a dot (e.g. ...\.temp\brain-sandbox) were invisible and AskAsync waited forever on a reply that had already landed. Fixed (regex [^a-zA-Z0-9] -> "-"), ClaudeConfigDiscovery now delegates to the same encoder, 6 regression tests added (Core.Tests). This also fixes Director transcript reads for any dotted repo path.

## Live verification (evidence: .temp/qa-184/*.png)

- Panel (Task Scheduler launch, real claude.exe): START HOST (spawned in 2.6s) -> ASK "Reply with exactly: BRAIN-OK-184" -> reply in 10.5s -> CLEAR (9.4s, new transcript id discovered file-level, no relink) -> RESTART (new pid) -> HEALTH alive -> KILL. All verbs pass.
- Gateway (test instance, :7979, CC_GATEWAY_NO_TAILSCALE=1, Task Scheduler launch): Settings shows "not started (spawns on first use)"; Restart Brain click -> "alive - Quiet, idle 3s" with session id + pid; POST /shutdown -> gateway AND its brain claude.exe both exit, no orphan.
- Production: installed tray gateway redeployed via --apply-update (outcome=Updated), healthz 200 on 0.6.6+123eb55, one-URL front door 200. The production brain stays dormant until its first consumer (#185).

Full solution builds clean; Gateway tests at parity. Changes are in the working tree, NOT committed (commits on explicit request only).

title: AgentBrain v2: warm brain hosted IN-PROCESS in the Gateway (on HostedAgent, REST transport retired)

body:
Two related symptoms observed live on soren-north (2026-06-05, v0.6.6, one-URL deployment):

1. The 443 front-door mapping (https://<tailnet>/ -> localhost:7878) VANISHED from the
   Tailscale serve table some time between 16:50 and 17:30, turning the one URL into a
   502 from every device. A Gateway restart re-asserted it (the provisioner only asserts
   the front door in Start()).

2. The serve table is accumulating ephemeral-port mappings (e.g. :50682, :61602, :62917,
   ... -> http://localhost:<same-port>), created by HandleAdded when hosted-agent /
   ephemeral-port Directors register. ReconcileOrphans only cleans the fixed Director
   range 7879-7898, so these never get removed.

Suspected root cause for (1): serve-table writes racing - every QueueServeOn/ServeOff
shells the tailscale CLI, and the hosted-agent work now registers many short-lived
Directors. Needs investigation: does `tailscale serve --https=P <target>` ever rewrite
the whole table? Are concurrent writes from multiple gateway instances possible?

Fix shape (to be confirmed by the investigation):
- Re-assert the front door periodically (or on every reconcile), not only at Start().
- Either stop mapping ephemeral-port Directors entirely, or extend orphan cleanup to
  any same-port mapping whose Director is gone (the provisioner-shape parse in
  ParseManagedHttpsPorts already identifies them).

Refs: docs/plans/one-url-cockpit.md, src/CcDirector.Gateway/Tailscale/TailscaleServeProvisioner.cs

comments.body:
Root-caused, fixed, and verified live on soren-north (2026-06-06).

## Root cause

**Pile-up (symptom 2):** HandleAdded mapped EVERY registered Director, including Directors on other machines. The laptop's hosted-agent Directors register as https://sorenlaptop...:54283 etc., and the provisioner created soren-north:54283 -> http://localhost:54283 - a proxy to a local port nothing listens on (502 from birth). ReconcileOrphans only swept 7879-7898, so these leaked forever: the serve table had grown to **308 entries, 307 of them provisioner-shaped garbage**. Amplifier: the laptop Director (7879) heartbeats every 15s but its control endpoint is unreachable from this machine, so the registry sweeper evicted it every ~3.5 min ("evicted unreachable http entry ... still heartbeating") -> serve off/on churn -> two full-table writes per cycle, all day.

**443 drop (symptom 1):** entries demonstrably vanish from the serve table without any cc-director process removing them. Hard evidence: gateway PID 79892 mapped --https=7879 at 16:50:15 (exit 0), then its own removal at 16:54:02 failed with "handler does not exist"; same pattern at 15:25 for 7886. Ruled out: `tailscale serve reset` (the 307 old entries survived the incident, so the table was never wholesale-wiped), the setup engine (never touches tailscale), the repo Debug gateway (runs CC_GATEWAY_NO_TAILSCALE=1), and overlapping gateway processes (yesterday's gateways were strictly sequential). Answers to the investigation questions: yes, every `tailscale serve` CLI call is a read-modify-write of the WHOLE ServeConfig through tailscaled's LocalAPI, and concurrent gateway instances are possible only in restart-overlap windows (the in-process lock does not span processes). The precise trigger inside tailscale 1.98.2 is not provable from our logs - which is why the fix is self-healing rather than trigger-chasing.

## Fix (TailscaleServeProvisioner)

1. **Map only local, fixed-range Directors.** A Director gets a mapping only when it runs on THIS machine (loopback control endpoint or matching MachineName) AND its port is in 7879-7898. Remote-machine and ephemeral-port (hosted-agent) Directors are never mapped - this also silences the eviction churn's serve writes.
2. **Periodic self-healing reconcile** (5-min timer + one pass at startup): one `serve status --json` read, then (a) re-assert 443 -> gateway when missing or pointing at the wrong backend, (b) re-assert any missing mapping for a live local Director, (c) remove provisioner-shaped same-port mappings on ANY port whose Director is gone (443 and foreign mappings are never touched).
3. 13 new keyless unit tests (TailscaleServeProvisionerTests) covering the mapping policy and the reconcile decision logic.

## Live verification

- Deployed to the installed tray Gateway via the --apply-update self-update path (outcome=Updated, .old backup in place), now running 0.6.6+97bc2475.
- First reconcile swept **302 orphans**; serve table went **304 -> 4 entries** (443 + 7884/7886/7887).
- Laptop Director re-registration now logs "not mapping ... only local Directors on ports 7879-7898" - no serve writes.
- Front door verified end-to-end: https://soren-north.taildb08ed.ts.net/healthz -> 200, Cockpit / -> 200.
- First periodic tick at +5min: "Reconcile: serve table consistent (front door + 3 Director mappings)".

Note: the fix is in the working tree, NOT yet committed (commits happen on explicit request only).

title: TailscaleServeProvisioner: 443 front door dropped from the serve table + ephemeral-port mapping pile-up

body:
## Problem / Value

A live v0.6.6 gateway install reported success but silently did **not** swap the gateway exe (cockpit extracted at 0.6.6, gateway left at the old build - a silent half-install). Root cause: the gateway self-update **test** run wrote FAKE versions into the **production** setup state:
- `%LOCALAPPDATA%\cc-director\config\setup\installed.json` -> `"gateway":"9.9.9"`
- `%LOCALAPPDATA%\cc-director\config\setup\update-pins.json` -> `"gateway":"9.9.8"`

`UpdatePlanner` then judged installed `9.9.9 >= release 0.6.6` -> UpToDate -> the swap never ran, while `GatewayTrayInstaller` restarted the OLD exe and reported success. Recovered manually by correcting `installed.json` to the exe's real version.

## Scope (two independent defenses)

**IN**
1. `scripts/test-gateway-selfupdate.ps1` (and any self-update test) MUST run against an isolated layout via a `--root` override, never the production `%LOCALAPPDATA%\cc-director` setup dir.
2. A sanity guard in `UpdatePlanner`: when the recorded installed version is higher than any released version (or disagrees sharply with the running exe's `FileVersion`), log loudly and prefer the exe's actual file stamp rather than silently reporting UpToDate.

**OUT**
- Redesigning the update manifest / versioning scheme.
- The SHA-skip download optimization (#178).

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Running `scripts/test-gateway-selfupdate.ps1` leaves the production `installed.json` and `update-pins.json` byte-identical to before the run. (Proof: hash before/after.)
- [ ] Given an `installed.json` recording a version higher than the newest release, `UpdatePlanner` logs a loud warning and does NOT report UpToDate based on it (prefers the exe `FileVersion`). (Proof: unit test on `UpdatePlanner` with a poisoned installed version.)
- [ ] A normal install/update still completes and reports success only when the exe was actually swapped. (Proof: install log showing the swap.)

## Affected Containers

- `CcDirector.Setup.Engine` - `UpdatePlanner.cs`, `GatewayTrayInstaller.cs`; `scripts/test-gateway-selfupdate.ps1`; `tools/cc-director-setup-engine.Tests`.

## Proof Target

`docs/cencon/proof/issue-176/`: before/after hashes of the production setup files across a test run + the green `UpdatePlanner` sanity-guard unit test.

## Assumptions

- An installed version strictly greater than the maximum released version is always anomalous (test pollution), never legitimate. None invented.

comments.body: Claimed by an implementation-loop run. Manual duplicate-prevention (#298 not built yet): removing flow:ready-dev so no concurrent loop selects this issue while implemented. Transitions to flow:ready-qa then flow:done per normal flow; flow:ready-dev restored if the run aborts.

## Developer Agent - ready for QA

Implemented both independent defenses from the spec.

**Release Notes:** Self-update test harness no longer pollutes production install state; UpdatePlanner no longer trusts an impossibly-high recorded version (eliminates the silent half-install).

**Changes**
1. `scripts/test-gateway-selfupdate.ps1`: `-Root` override + `CC_DIRECTOR_ROOT` isolation, production-root refusal, before/after hash check of production setup files.
2. `UpdatePlanner` sanity guard: `InstalledComponent` carries the exe's real `FileVersion`; the planner detects recorded > newest-release (pollution signature), logs loudly, and prefers the exe stamp over reporting UpToDate.

**How to Test:** `dotnet test tools/cc-director-setup-engine.Tests/CcDirector.Setup.Engine.Tests.csproj` -> 153/153 green (4 new guard tests + 2 new isolation tests).

**Expected Result**
- AC1: production `installed.json`/`update-pins.json` byte-identical across a run (hashes in proof).
